proposals.title: Deconstructing Intrinsic Capacity: A Psychometric Factor Analysis to Determine Its Latent Structure
proposals.motivation: Intrinsic capacity (IC), a core construct of the WHO's healthy aging framework, is theoretically defined across five domains (locomotion, vitality, sensory, cognitive, psychological). However, its operationalization and measurement lack empirical grounding, often relying on a priori assumptions of a simple, unidimensional or bidimensional structure. This ambiguity hinders the development of valid assessment tools and the creation of targeted interventions for maintaining functional ability in older adults, a critical global health priority pre-2026.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that intrinsic capacity is not a simple, unidimensional construct but possesses a more complex, hierarchical latent structure, analogous to the evolution of intelligence models, which can be revealed through rigorous psychometric analysis of a comprehensive measurement battery.
proposals.method: We will recruit a large, diverse sample of older adults (e.g., aged 60+). A broad battery of validated measures covering all five IC domains (e.g., gait speed, grip strength, MoCA, GDS-15, hearing/vision tests) will be administered. We will first conduct Exploratory Factor Analysis (EFA) using principal axis factoring with oblique rotation (Promax) to identify the underlying factor structure. Subsequently, we will perform Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA) using structural equation modeling (e.g., in Mplus or R's lavaan) to test and compare competing models (unidimensional, correlated factors, hierarchical) for model fit, using indices like CFI, TLI, RMSEA, and SRMR.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first robust, data-driven empirical model of intrinsic capacity's latent structure, moving the field beyond theoretical speculation. It will directly inform the development of a psychometrically sound, concise measurement instrument for IC, analogous to the Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI) developed for stigma research. This foundational work is crucial for accurate assessment, monitoring, and intervention in aging populations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a classic psychometric paradigm from intelligence research—using factor analysis to challenge oversimplified models—to the nascent construct of intrinsic capacity, a method not yet systematically employed in this domain. It leverages Dr. Griffiths's expertise in cross-cultural instrument validation and factor analysis (e.g., on WeSEI and Nomophobia) to solve a structural problem in gerontology.

proposals.title: Beyond Two Factors: A Digital Phenotyping Study to Uncover the Latent Structure of Intrinsic Capacity in Older Adults
proposals.motivation: The WHO's intrinsic capacity (IC) framework is a crucial concept for healthy aging, but its operationalization is often oversimplified into two-factor models (e.g., physical and cognitive) based on sparse, self-reported data. This fails to capture the complex, multidimensional nature of functional capacity as expressed in daily life. Concurrently, digital phenotyping via smartphones offers rich, continuous behavioral data but is predominantly used to measure problematic use, not underlying capacity.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying latent variable modeling to multidimensional digital phenotyping data will reveal a more complex, empirically-grounded factor structure of intrinsic capacity than traditional two-factor models, identifying latent dimensions such as cognitive resilience, socio-emotional regulation, and motivational drive.
proposals.method: We will recruit a cohort of older adults (aged 60+) to install a smartphone sensing app collecting passive data on mobility (GPS), communication (call/SMS logs), app usage sequences, and screen time patterns over 3 months. Following Griffiths' expertise in psychometric validation, we will extract features (e.g., circadian rhythm regularity, social network entropy, app diversity) and apply exploratory factor analysis (EFA) and confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) to identify latent dimensions. Measurement invariance testing will assess factor stability across key subgroups (e.g., by gender, health status).
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first empirically-derived, multidimensional model of intrinsic capacity grounded in real-world behavior. It will develop and validate a novel digital biomarker framework for gerontology, shifting smartphone data from a stigma-laden metric of overuse to a tool for capacity assessment. The findings will challenge oversimplified IC models and inform more personalized aging interventions.
proposals.novelty: This fuses the conceptual critique of IC models with the methodological rigor of psychometrics (factor analysis, invariance testing) applied to digital phenotyping data, repurposing smartphone sensing from detecting pathology to profiling fundamental human capacity.

proposals.title: Culturally Augmenting ICOPE: A Community-Based Participatory Study of Healthy Aging Models in Malaysia and Indonesia
proposals.motivation: The WHO's Integrated Care for Older People (ICOPE) framework provides a universal etic model for assessing intrinsic capacity, but its applicability across diverse cultures is not fully validated. Pre-2026, there is growing recognition that health metrics developed in Western contexts may not capture culturally specific domains of well-being, leading to measurement non-invariance and potentially ineffective interventions. This is critical for global aging policy, as mismatched frameworks can perpetuate health inequities.
proposals.hypothesis: A culturally augmented version of the ICOPE framework, integrating locally derived emic constructs of healthy aging, will demonstrate superior predictive validity for health-related quality of life and well-being outcomes compared to the standard ICOPE framework alone in non-Western populations.
proposals.method: Employing a community-based participatory design, Phase 1 will conduct focus groups and free-listing exercises in Malaysian and Indonesian communities to inductively map local models of healthy aging. Phase 2 will use thematic analysis to derive emic constructs and systematically compare them with ICOPE's etic domains (cognition, mobility, etc.). Phase 3 will develop a hybrid assessment tool, integrating new culturally specific domains (e.g., social harmony, spiritual connectedness). Its psychometric properties, including measurement invariance across gender and rural/urban settings, will be evaluated using confirmatory factor analysis (CFA). Phase 4 will test predictive validity via hierarchical regression, comparing the hybrid and standard models' ability to predict outcomes like depression (PHQ-9) and life satisfaction (SWLS).
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a validated, culturally augmented ICOPE tool for Southeast Asian contexts, directly addressing measurement gaps. It will advance methodological rigor in cross-cultural gerontology by demonstrating a systematic process for integrating emic and etic models. The findings will provide evidence-based recommendations for making global aging frameworks more equitable and effective.
proposals.novelty: While cross-cultural validation is common, this study moves beyond simple translation/adaptation to a participatory, inductive co-design of the framework itself. It uniquely applies advanced psychometric methods (e.g., measurement invariance testing) from stigma and behavioral addiction research to the novel domain of intrinsic capacity assessment.

proposals.title: Psychometric Evaluation of the WHO ICOPE Screening Tool: Testing Measurement Invariance and Sensitivity to Intervention Across Diverse Cultural and Weight-Stigma Contexts
proposals.motivation: The WHO's Integrated Care for Older People (ICOPE) framework is a crucial global initiative for healthy aging, but its screening tools require rigorous validation beyond Western populations to ensure cultural fairness. Pre-2026, the lack of validated, culturally responsive measures for geriatric care risks misdiagnosis and inequitable program evaluation. This study addresses this gap by integrating principles from weight stigma measurement (WeSEI) to examine how sociocultural factors, including stigma, influence the assessment of intrinsic capacity.
proposals.hypothesis: The factorial structure of the ICOPE screening tool will demonstrate configural and metric invariance across culturally diverse groups and levels of weight-stigma exposure, and post-intervention score changes will reflect true intervention effects rather than measurement bias.
proposals.method: A longitudinal, multi-site design will be implemented in at least three non-Western countries (e.g., Indonesia, Malaysia, and a third Asian or African site). Participants (older adults, n≈1200) will complete the ICOPE tool, the Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI), and measures of mattering and psychosocial well-being at baseline and after a standardized, evidence-based geriatric intervention. Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA) and Multi-Group CFA (MG-CFA) will be used to test for measurement invariance (configural, metric, scalar) across cultural sites, gender, and levels of stigma exposure pre- and post-intervention. Latent Change Score modeling will then assess if intervention-induced changes are invariant across groups.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce the first cross-culturally validated and intervention-sensitive version of the ICOPE screening tool, directly supporting global aging policy. It will advance methodological understanding of how stigma and culture interact with measurement in geriatric assessment, providing a model for validating other health frameworks across diverse populations.
proposals.novelty: This study uniquely fuses cultural validation with intervention responsiveness testing, applying advanced psychometric methods (invariance testing of change scores) from digital behavior research (e.g., nomophobia) to the geriatric care domain. It innovatively examines weight stigma as a potential moderator of measurement fairness in a non-weight-related health context.

proposals.title: Reconceptualizing the ICOPES-TW as a Formative Index: A Psychometric Redesign for Enhanced Coherence and Predictive Validity in Weight Stigma Research
proposals.motivation: The Interpersonal Coping Responses to Weight Stigma (ICOPES-TW) scale is a key tool for understanding how individuals manage weight-based discrimination. However, its validation as a reflective scale, where items are interchangeable indicators of a latent trait, may be psychometrically and conceptually flawed. Pre-2026 literature on formative measurement (e.g., Bollen & Diamantopoulos, 2008; Coltman et al., 2008) argues that constructs defined by distinct, non-interchangeable coping strategies are better modeled as indices, where items cause the composite score. This misalignment threatens the scale's validity and utility in predicting outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the ICOPES-TW is more coherently and validly modeled as a formative index. Consequently, a psychometric redesign guided by this formative conceptualization will yield an instrument with superior content coverage and stronger predictive validity for key outcomes (e.g., psychological distress, health behaviors) compared to the original reflective model.
proposals.method: The study will employ a multi-phase, mixed-methods design. First, a systematic content analysis of existing ICOPES-TW items and a review of the weight stigma coping literature will identify distinct, necessary facets of the construct. New items will be developed to fill gaps. Second, data will be collected from a large sample (N > 500) of individuals with diverse body weights. Third, psychometric evaluation will use confirmatory tetrad analysis (CTA) to statistically test the reflective vs. formative model. The formative index will be validated by examining its nomological network, correlating it with established measures of weight stigma exposure (e.g., WeSEI), psychological well-being, and behavioral outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a psychometrically defensible version of the ICOPES-TW, clarifying the theoretical structure of coping responses to weight stigma. It will offer a methodological template for reconceptualizing other psychological scales where formative models are more appropriate. Ultimately, it will deliver a more valid tool for researchers and clinicians to assess how specific coping strategies collectively influence health outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While formative measurement is established in other fields, its application to reconceptualize an existing, widely used scale in weight stigma research is novel. The project moves beyond merely validating a scale to fundamentally re-evaluating its theoretical measurement model, a step rarely taken in this domain pre-2026.

proposals.title: Psychometric Validation and Invariance Testing of the ICOPES-TW: A Multi-Group Confirmatory Factor Analysis and Competitive Model Comparison
proposals.motivation: The Inventory of Controlling and Protective Eating Strategies - Thinness and Weight (ICOPES-TW) is a key tool for assessing cognitive strategies in eating pathology, but its proposed two-factor structure requires rigorous validation in diverse populations. Pre-2026, establishing measurement invariance is critical to ensure scores are comparable across groups, a methodological standard exemplified in recent work on the Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI). Without such validation, group comparisons in clinical and research settings may be invalid.
proposals.hypothesis: The hypothesized two-factor structure of the ICOPES-TW will demonstrate superior model fit compared to unidimensional and bifactor alternatives, and will exhibit full measurement invariance (configural, metric, and scalar) across gender and clinical status subgroups.
proposals.method: Using data from a large, diverse sample, we will first conduct a multi-group confirmatory factor analysis (MG-CFA) in Mplus or R's lavaan package to test for configural, metric, and scalar invariance across gender (male/female) and clinical status (clinical eating disorder vs. community). Subsequently, we will employ a competitive model testing framework, using robust maximum likelihood estimation and fit indices (CFI, TLI, RMSEA, SRMR), to statistically compare the fit of the hypothesized two-factor model against a unidimensional model and a bifactor model with a general factor and two specific factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive test of the ICOPES-TW's factor structure across key subgroups, directly addressing issues of generalizability. It will yield robust evidence for the scale's structural validity and establish whether it measures the same constructs equivalently across different populations, a prerequisite for its use in etiological and treatment research.
proposals.novelty: While invariance testing is established, its application to the ICOPES-TW is novel. More significantly, combining rigorous multi-group CFA with a direct, statistical comparison against plausible alternative models (like a bifactor model) moves beyond simple model fit assessment to provide stronger evidence for the specificity and superiority of the intended factor structure.

proposals.title: Validating the Two-Factor Structure of the ICOPES-TW: A Multi-Group Confirmatory Factor Analysis for Measurement Invariance Across Healthcare Networks
proposals.motivation: Valid psychometric instruments are crucial for accurately assessing patient-reported outcomes in clinical settings. The ICOPES-TW, designed to measure specific patient experiences, requires robust validation to ensure its two-factor structure is not sample-specific and can be reliably applied across diverse healthcare populations. Establishing measurement invariance is a prerequisite for meaningful group comparisons, yet it is often overlooked in initial validation studies, limiting an instrument's practical utility.
proposals.hypothesis: The two-factor structure of the ICOPES-TW instrument will demonstrate configural, metric, and scalar measurement invariance across two or three distinct sub-samples drawn from different regional healthcare networks. This will confirm that the theoretical model is robust and generalizable beyond a single sample.
proposals.method: Data will be collected from three independent samples of patients across distinct regional hospital systems or care networks. Multi-Group Confirmatory Factor Analysis (MGCFA) will be conducted using structural equation modeling software (e.g., Mplus or the lavaan package in R). The analysis will follow a hierarchical sequence of invariance testing: first testing configural invariance (same factor structure), then metric invariance (equal factor loadings), and finally scalar invariance (equal item intercepts). Model fit will be evaluated using pre-2026 standard indices (CFI, TLI, RMSEA, SRMR), with changes in CFI (ΔCFI ≤ -0.010) and RMSEA (ΔRMSEA ≥ 0.015) used to assess invariance.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide rigorous evidence for the structural validity and generalizability of the ICOPES-TW, strengthening its foundation for use in diverse clinical and research settings. It will offer a methodological template for validating other health psychology instruments by emphasizing cross-sample robustness, moving beyond single-sample confirmatory factor analysis. The findings will directly inform researchers and clinicians about the instrument's suitability for comparing scores across different patient groups.
proposals.novelty: The approach innovatively adapts the engineering principle of validating a theoretical model across multiple production batches to psychometrics, applying rigorous MGCFA invariance testing from the outset of an instrument's validation. This moves beyond the common practice of confirming a factor structure in a single new sample, providing a stronger, more practical test of structural robustness inspired by cross-disciplinary methods.

proposals.title: Confirmatory Factor Analysis of the Indonesian Coping with Weight Stigma Scale (ICOPES-TW): Validating the Two-Factor Structure in a Novel Sample
proposals.motivation: Weight stigma is a pervasive public health issue with severe psychological and physical consequences. Validated instruments to measure how individuals cope with this stigma are crucial for research and intervention. The Indonesian Coping with Weight Stigma Scale (ICOPES-TW) was developed to assess coping strategies, but its initial validation primarily used exploratory methods, necessitating a rigorous confirmatory test of its proposed two-factor structure.
proposals.hypothesis: The two-factor structure of the ICOPES-TW (comprising 'Appearance-based coping' and 'Weight-based coping' factors) will demonstrate a good model fit in an independent sample of Indonesian adults, confirming its construct validity. Measurement invariance will be established across gender and body mass index (BMI) categories.
proposals.method: We will recruit a new sample of Indonesian adults (N > 500) and administer the ICOPES-TW. Using confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) with maximum likelihood estimation in software like Mplus or the lavaan package in R, we will test the fit of the hypothesized two-factor model against alternative models (e.g., a one-factor model). Specific fit indices (CFI > .90, TLI > .90, RMSEA < .08, SRMR < .08) will be used to evaluate model adequacy. Subsequently, multi-group CFA will be conducted to test for measurement invariance across gender and BMI groups.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust psychometric evidence for the ICOPES-TW, solidifying it as a key tool for research on weight stigma coping in Indonesia. Establishing measurement invariance will ensure the scale's scores are comparable across important demographic subgroups, enabling more nuanced population-level studies and evaluations of stigma-reduction interventions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Griffiths' team has validated exposure inventories (WeSEI), this study applies rigorous CFA specifically to a coping scale in the Indonesian context, moving beyond exploratory validation to confirmatory testing and invariance analysis, which is a critical next step for instrument development.

proposals.title: Diagnostic Validation and Model-Appropriate Revision of the ICOPES-TW: A Study of Measurement Structure and Clinical Utility
proposals.motivation: The Internalized Coping with Obesity and Perceived Stigma Scale for Taiwanese (ICOPES-TW) is a crucial tool for assessing the psychological impact of weight stigma in a specific cultural context. However, its reported low internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha) poses a significant psychometric challenge, potentially leading to its misuse or abandonment in research and clinical practice. Addressing this requires moving beyond simply adding or removing items to first diagnose whether the construct is inherently multidimensional (formative) or a unidimensional latent trait (reflective), a fundamental pre-2026 measurement issue.
proposals.hypothesis: The low internal consistency of the ICOPES-TW stems from a misalignment between its scoring (as a reflective scale) and its true theoretical structure, which is hypothesized to be a formative, multidimensional construct. A model-appropriate revision will yield a tool with superior psychometric properties and stronger predictive validity for key outcomes like depression, anxiety, and self-esteem.
proposals.method: The study will employ a mixed-methods design. First, structural equation modeling (SEM) will be used to test competing measurement models (reflective vs. formative) using data from a large sample of Taiwanese adults with diverse body weights. Concurrently, a qualitative expert analysis with clinicians and stigma researchers will assess item content and theoretical coherence. Based on results, the scale will be revised: if formative, a composite index with weighted indicators will be developed; if reflective with flawed items, a theory-driven item revision will occur. The final scale will be validated against external criteria (e.g., DASS-21, Rosenberg Self-Esteem Scale) to ensure clinical relevance.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a validated, psychometrically sound version of the ICOPES-TW for use in Taiwanese populations, advancing cross-cultural stigma research. It will offer a methodological template for diagnosing and revising scales with low internal consistency, moving the field beyond a reflexive reliance on Cronbach's alpha. The output will be a clinically useful tool that accurately captures the complexity of internalized weight stigma.
proposals.novelty: Unlike typical scale validation studies that seek to maximize alpha, this project first diagnostically tests the theoretical measurement model itself. It integrates formative index construction—a method validated in Dr. Griffiths' prior work on the WeSEI—into the revision of a coping-focused scale, applying a novel, diagnosis-driven approach to scale development.

proposals.title: Predictive Validity of the Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI) for Clinical Health Outcomes: A Longitudinal Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research establishes weight stigma as a significant psychosocial stressor linked to adverse mental health outcomes and maladaptive health behaviors. However, its role as a prospective risk factor for hard clinical endpoints, such as functional decline, hospitalization, and mortality, remains underexplored. Establishing this predictive link is critical to elevate weight stigma from a social justice concern to a recognized public health priority requiring clinical intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: Higher baseline scores on the Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI) will significantly predict greater future functional decline, increased rates of hospitalization, and higher all-cause mortality over a multi-year follow-up period, even after controlling for baseline BMI, comorbidities, and socioeconomic status.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective longitudinal cohort study, recruiting a large sample of adults with diverse body weights. Baseline assessment will include the WeSEI, demographic/clinical covariates, and a measure of functional status (e.g., SF-36 Physical Functioning scale). Participants will be followed for a minimum of three years via linked electronic health records and annual surveys. Predictive validity will be tested using Cox proportional hazards regression models for time-to-event outcomes (hospitalization, mortality) and linear mixed-effects models for repeated measures of functional decline, with WeSEI score as the primary predictor.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal evidence directly linking quantified exposure to weight stigma to consequential downstream health outcomes. It will validate the WeSEI not only as a psychometric tool but as a clinically significant risk stratification instrument. The findings aim to inform the development of stigma-reduction interventions and integrated care pathways that address stigma as a modifiable determinant of health.
proposals.novelty: While prior work validates the WeSEI's factor structure and cross-cultural invariance, and correlates stigma with concurrent psychological distress, no pre-2026 study has tested its capacity to forecast objective, long-term clinical trajectories. This moves beyond cross-sectional correlation to establish temporal and predictive precedence.

proposals.title: Establishing Population Norms and Clinically Significant Cut-Offs for the Indonesian Compulsive Online Problematic Experiences Scale – Thinness/Wrestling (ICOPES-TW) Across Age Groups
proposals.motivation: The ICOPES-TW, developed pre-2026, measures problematic engagement with online content promoting extreme thinness and 'thinspiration' or 'fitspiration' (TW). While validated, the lack of population norms and clinically meaningful cut-off scores limits its utility for screening and public health surveillance. This is critical as exposure to such content is a known risk factor for eating disorders and body image disturbances, particularly among adolescents and young adults.
proposals.hypothesis: That population norms for ICOPES-TW scores will differ significantly across adolescent, young adult, and adult age groups, and that empirically derived cut-off scores can be established to reliably distinguish between normative and clinically significant problematic use.
proposals.method: We will conduct a large-scale, nationally representative survey in Indonesia, stratified by age (adolescents: 13-17, young adults: 18-25, adults: 26-40). Data will be analyzed using confirmatory factor analysis to verify measurement invariance across groups, a prerequisite for valid norm comparisons. Normative data (means, standard deviations, percentile ranks) will be calculated for each group. Cut-off scores will be established using receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve analysis, using a validated external criterion (e.g., a structured clinical interview for disordered eating or the Eating Disorder Examination-Questionnaire) to determine optimal sensitivity and specificity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first population-based reference data for the ICOPES-TW, enabling clinicians and researchers to interpret individual scores in context. The empirically derived cut-offs will transform the scale from a research tool into a practical screening instrument for identifying individuals at high risk, facilitating early intervention and more accurate epidemiological tracking of this online behavioral problem.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Griffiths' team has established norms for tools like the WeSEI, applying this methodology to a novel, culturally specific scale measuring a behavioral addiction (ICOPES-TW) and generating age-stratified clinical thresholds represents a significant advancement beyond simple psychometric validation.

proposals.title: Validating the ICOPES-TW as a Holistic Outcome Measure: An Ecological Monitoring Framework for Assessing Geriatric Intervention Sensitivity
proposals.motivation: Evaluating the effectiveness of geriatric interventions requires tools that can sensitively capture changes across the complex, multi-domain health of older adults. Pre-2026, while comprehensive geriatric assessment tools like the ICOPES-TW exist, their validation often focuses on cross-sectional psychometrics rather than longitudinal sensitivity to change. This gap limits evidence-based refinement of care programs and rehabilitation strategies aimed at improving holistic well-being in aging populations.
proposals.hypothesis: The ICOPES-TW total and domain scores will demonstrate statistically significant and meaningful pre-post changes in response to a standardized geriatric intervention, and the pattern of these changes will align with the intervention's targeted health domains, confirming the tool's sensitivity as an outcome measure.
proposals.method: The study will employ a pre-post repeated measures design, adapting the Before-After Control-Impact (BACI) framework from ecological monitoring. A sample of older adults will undergo a baseline ICOPES-TW assessment. Following a standardized, multi-component geriatric intervention (e.g., a 12-week integrated physical-cognitive-social program), participants will complete the ICOPES-TW at multiple post-intervention time points. The core analysis will use Mixed Model Repeated Measures (MMRM) to test for significant time-by-intervention effects on the total score. Secondary analyses will involve Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA) to test for measurement invariance across time points and calculate standardized effect sizes (e.g., Cohen's d) for each ICOPES-TW domain to assess differential sensitivity.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first longitudinal validation of the ICOPES-TW's sensitivity to change, establishing it as a robust outcome measure for geriatric care. It will introduce a novel analytical framework from environmental science to gerontology, offering a rigorous method for evaluating multi-domain health interventions. The findings will directly inform clinical practice by identifying which holistic health domains are most responsive to specific interventions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conceptual and methodological translation of an ecological monitoring framework (BACI) to geriatric outcome measurement, a cross-disciplinary approach not previously applied in this context. It moves beyond standard psychometric validation to explicitly test an instrument's dynamic responsiveness, analogous to detecting ecosystem recovery.

proposals.title: Mapping the Dynamics of Intervention Response: An Intensive Longitudinal Mixed-Methods Study of Weight Stigma and Geriatric Care in High-Risk Older Adults
proposals.motivation: Weight stigma is a significant psychosocial stressor linked to adverse health outcomes, and its impact on older adults, particularly those with complex health needs, is under-researched. Standardized geriatric interventions often fail to account for the unique, person-specific pathways through which stigma influences treatment engagement and effectiveness, creating a critical gap in person-centered care models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that intensive longitudinal case studies will reveal distinct, idiosyncratic temporal patterns linking exposure to weight stigma (as measured by the WeSEI) to fluctuations in clinical outcomes (measured by ICOPES-TW) during a geriatric intervention, with these patterns being mediated by personal and social factors such as perceived mattering and social support.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of N-of-1, repeated-measures mixed-methods case studies on 6-8 high-risk geriatric patients with high Weight-Related Self-Stigma (WRSS). Participants will undergo a standardized geriatric care program (e.g., ICOPE). Quantitative data will be collected daily via the ICOPES-TW and weekly via the WeSEI and mattering scales. Concurrent qualitative data from weekly semi-structured interviews and clinical observations will be collected. Data fusion will involve time-series analysis of quantitative scores (e.g., using dynamic factor models) followed by qualitative thematic analysis, with integration via a joint display to contextualize score fluctuations within personal narratives and observed clinical events.
proposals.contribution: This study will generate novel, mechanistic hypotheses about how weight stigma dynamically interferes with or facilitates geriatric care at the individual level. It will provide a detailed methodological template for fusing intensive longitudinal assessment with qualitative contextualization in health psychology, moving beyond group-level averages to understand person-specific intervention pathways.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in applying an intensive longitudinal, person-centered design—common in digital mental health—to the understudied intersection of weight stigma and geriatric care, explicitly modeling stigma exposure as a time-varying predictor of daily functional outcomes within a treatment context.

proposals.title: Assessing the Responsiveness of the ICOPES-TW to Geriatric Care Interventions: A Longitudinal Validation Study
proposals.motivation: The ICOPES-TW (Integrated Care for Older People Screening Tool-Taiwan) is a key instrument for identifying care needs in older adults. To be clinically useful for evaluating program effectiveness, it must demonstrate responsiveness—the ability to detect meaningful change over time following an intervention. Pre-2026, while its psychometric properties like factor structure and invariance have been studied, its sensitivity to change remains unvalidated, limiting its application in outcome measurement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ICOPES-TW total and subscale scores will show statistically significant and clinically meaningful improvements (decreases in need scores) from pre- to post-intervention among older adults participating in structured geriatric care programs, and that these changes will be greater than those observed in a matched comparison group receiving usual care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, longitudinal study with a quasi-experimental design. Older adults (n=300) enrolled in a multi-component geriatric intervention (e.g., comprehensive geriatric assessment, care coordination, exercise) will complete the ICOPES-TW at baseline, 3-month, and 6-month follow-ups. A matched control group (n=150) will complete the same assessments. Responsiveness will be evaluated using paired t-tests/Wilcoxon tests for within-group change and ANCOVA for between-group differences. We will calculate standardized response means (SRM) and Guyatt's responsiveness index to quantify sensitivity, and use confirmatory factor analysis to test for longitudinal measurement invariance to ensure score changes reflect true change, not measurement artifact.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence for the ICOPES-TW's responsiveness, a critical psychometric property for outcome measures. It will validate the tool for use in program evaluation and clinical trials within geriatric care. The findings will also inform the minimal clinically important difference (MCID) for the ICOPES-TW, offering a benchmark for interpreting score changes in practice.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Griffiths and others has focused on validating stigma exposure inventories (e.g., WeSEI) and establishing cross-sectional invariance, this study uniquely applies these advanced psychometric methods—specifically longitudinal invariance testing and responsiveness indices—to a geriatric screening tool, bridging methodological expertise from behavioral addictions/weight stigma to aging research.

proposals.title: Explainable and Causal Machine Learning for Geriatric Risk Stratification: Integrating ICOPES-TW with Clinical and Social Determinants to Predict Adverse Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Geriatric risk assessment tools often lack transparency, making it difficult for clinicians to trust and act upon their predictions. The ICOPES-TW (Interdisciplinary Comprehensive Geriatric Assessment and Care Planning for Taiwan) framework captures multidimensional health data, but its integration with other clinical and social determinants via black-box models limits clinical utility. Pre-2026, there is a pressing need for predictive systems that provide clear, causal explanations to support personalized intervention planning in complex older adult populations.
proposals.hypothesis: An inherently interpretable machine learning model, integrating ICOPES-TW data with clinical and social determinants, can generate clinically plausible, causally-attributed risk predictions for adverse geriatric outcomes (e.g., hospitalization, functional decline) that outperform or match black-box models while providing actionable explanatory insights.
proposals.method: We will employ Generalized Additive Models (GAMs) with pairwise interactions or Explainable Boosting Machines (EBMs), which provide clear feature attributions. Model inputs will include structured ICOPES-TW domain scores, lab values, medication counts, and validated social determinant measures (e.g., socioeconomic status, social support). We will use causal discovery algorithms, such as the PC algorithm or Fast Causal Inference (FCI), on observational data to propose potential causal relationships between predictors and outcomes. These causal graphs will be validated for clinical plausibility against a structured knowledge base derived from pre-2026 geriatric medicine guidelines and systematic reviews.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, transparent risk stratification tool specifically for geriatric care. It will contribute a methodological framework for integrating complex assessment data with causal reasoning in clinical prediction. The output will be a set of interpretable, factor-specific risk attributions that clinicians can use to prioritize targeted interventions for individual patients.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of causal discovery methods with inherently interpretable ML models for geriatric prediction, moving beyond post-hoc explanation. Furthermore, it uniquely validates the model's reasoning against a formalized geriatric knowledge base to ensure clinical trustworthiness, a step not commonly emphasized in pre-2026 clinical ML research.

proposals.title: Psychometric Evaluation and Adaptation of the Internalized Coping with Obesity and Perceived Stigma Scale for Tweens (ICOPES-TW) for Younger At-Risk Adolescents: Measurement Invariance Across Weight Status and Gender
proposals.motivation: Weight stigma is a significant psychosocial stressor linked to adverse mental and physical health outcomes, with adolescence being a critical period for its internalization. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Griffiths' work on the WeSEI, has established the need to measure stigma exposure, but validated tools for assessing the internalization of weight stigma in younger, at-risk adolescent populations (e.g., those with early-onset chronic conditions or higher weight status) are lacking. Early identification of internalized stigma in this demographic is crucial for timely psychological intervention to prevent long-term sequelae.
proposals.hypothesis: The ICOPES-TW can be successfully adapted and validated for use with younger adolescents (aged 12-14) at elevated risk for weight-related stigma. The adapted scale will demonstrate strong psychometric properties (factorial validity, reliability) and measurement invariance across gender and weight status categories.
proposals.method: The study will employ a cross-sectional, survey-based design. Following best practices for scale adaptation, the ICOPES-TW items will undergo linguistic and cognitive debriefing with the target age group. Data will be collected from a large sample (N > 500) of adolescents aged 12-14, recruited from schools and pediatric clinics. Analyses will use Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA) to test the pre-existing factor structure, with Multi-Group CFA (MG-CFA) to establish measurement invariance across gender (male/female) and weight status (based on BMI percentile). Internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha) and convergent validity analyses with measures of self-esteem and depression will also be conducted.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first validated tool specifically designed to measure internalized weight stigma in younger at-risk adolescents, filling a critical gap in assessment. It will enable researchers and clinicians to identify early signs of stigma internalization, facilitating targeted interventions. Furthermore, establishing measurement invariance will ensure the scale's scores are comparable across key subgroups, supporting its use in diverse research and clinical settings.
proposals.novelty: While the WeSEI measures exposure to stigma, this project focuses on the critical, understudied outcome of internalization in a younger developmental period. Adapting a scale designed for middle-aged adults to a younger adolescent population, with rigorous testing for invariance, is a novel application of psychometric methods within weight stigma research.

proposals.title: Development and Psychometric Validation of a Caregiver-Proxy Version of the Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI-C) for Older Adults
proposals.motivation: Weight stigma is a significant psychosocial stressor linked to adverse health outcomes. For older adults with cognitive or communicative impairments, self-report of stigma exposure can be unreliable or impossible, creating a critical measurement gap. A validated caregiver-proxy instrument is needed to assess this exposure in vulnerable populations, enabling research on its prevalence and impact on health and well-being in later life.
proposals.hypothesis: The Caregiver-Proxy Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI-C) will demonstrate strong psychometric properties (internal consistency, test-retest reliability, and construct validity) and will show significant, positive correlations with older adults' self-reported stigma (where possible) and related indicators of psychological distress.
proposals.method: The study will adapt the original WeSEI items into a proxy-report format. We will recruit a dyadic sample of community-dwelling older adults and their primary caregivers. Using factor analysis (confirmatory and exploratory), we will examine the structural validity and measurement invariance of the WeSEI-C across different caregiver relationships and older adult health statuses. Convergent and discriminant validity will be assessed by correlating WeSEI-C scores with established measures of caregiver stress, older adult depression/anxiety, and health-related quality of life.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated tool for researchers and clinicians to systematically assess weight stigma exposure in older adults who cannot self-report. It will expand the application of Dr. Griffiths's WeSEI into gerontology and caregiving research, facilitating studies on the role of stigma in age-related health disparities and caregiver burden.
proposals.novelty: While proxy measures exist for functional capacity, developing one for a subjective, psychosocial experience like weight stigma is a novel and methodologically challenging extension of existing work. It applies the rigorous psychometric approach from Dr. Griffiths's prior invariance studies to a new population and respondent type.

proposals.title: A Bidirectional Revision of the ICOPES-TW: Psychometric Development and Longitudinal Testing of Reciprocal Distress-Problematic Social Media Use Pathways
proposals.motivation: The Internet Cognitions and Psychopathology – Escapism Scale for Social Networking Sites (ICOPES-TW) is a key tool for assessing problematic social media use (PSMU) driven by distress. However, its psychometric properties, particularly internal consistency, require improvement. Furthermore, its theoretical underpinning of a unidirectional distress-to-use pathway is increasingly challenged by evidence suggesting PSMU can also exacerbate distress, creating a potential reinforcing cycle that existing cross-sectional, unidirectional instruments cannot adequately capture.
proposals.hypothesis: That psychological distress and problematic social media use are linked via bidirectional, reciprocal pathways, which can be reliably measured by distinct subscales of a revised instrument and will demonstrate significant cross-lagged effects in longitudinal data.
proposals.method: The study will first revise the ICOPES-TW through expert review and cognitive interviews to generate new items explicitly capturing distress-as-antecedent and distress-as-consequence of PSMU. Using survey data from young adults, we will conduct exploratory and confirmatory factor analyses (EFA/CFA) to establish the factor structure, internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha, composite reliability), and convergent/discriminant validity of the new bidirectional subscales. The validated instrument will then be deployed in a three-wave longitudinal panel study over six months. Cross-lagged panel models (CLPM) will be the primary statistical architecture to test for reciprocal temporal relationships between the subscale scores.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the field with a psychometrically robust, theory-driven instrument capable of disentangling the directional components of the distress-PSMU relationship. It will move beyond correlation to offer empirical, longitudinal evidence for or against the hypothesized reciprocal cycle, informing more nuanced etiological models and targeted interventions.
proposals.novelty: This work fuses rigorous psychometric scale development—applying methods akin to those used in the WeSEI validation—with advanced longitudinal modeling to explicitly test bidirectional causality within the ICOPES framework, a significant advancement over its original unidirectional conceptualization and typical cross-sectional validation studies.

proposals.title: Cross-Cultural and Lifespan Validation of the Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI): Assessing Measurement Invariance Across Diverse Adult Populations
proposals.motivation: Weight stigma is a global public health concern, but its measurement remains largely validated in Western and specific East Asian (e.g., Taiwanese, Chinese) young adult and adolescent samples. Pre-2026, the WeSEI has shown promise in Indonesia and Malaysia, but its factor structure and psychometric properties are untested in broader cultural contexts (e.g., South Asian, Middle Eastern, European) and across the adult lifespan. Valid measurement is a prerequisite for comparative research and understanding the universality versus cultural specificity of weight stigma experiences.
proposals.hypothesis: The three-factor structure of the WeSEI (teasing, exclusion, and negative treatment) will demonstrate configural and metric invariance across diverse cultural groups and age cohorts, but scalar invariance may be limited, indicating cultural differences in the interpretation or threshold of stigma experiences.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-national survey using translated and back-translated versions of the WeSEI, administered to adults (N≈3000) stratified by age (18-30, 31-50, 51+) from six distinct cultural regions: the UK, Japan, Saudi Arabia, India, Germany, and Brazil. Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA) will test the original three-factor model in each group. Multi-group CFA (MG-CFA) will then be employed to test for measurement invariance across cultural groups and age cohorts, sequentially constraining factor loadings (metric), intercepts (scalar), and residual variances (strict). Analyses will use robust maximum likelihood estimation in Mplus 8.0 to handle non-normal data.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive evidence for the cross-cultural and age-related applicability of the WeSEI, establishing its utility for global research on weight stigma. It will identify potential items requiring cultural adaptation and generate population-specific normative data, enabling more accurate between-group comparisons and the development of targeted anti-stigma interventions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work validated the WeSEI in specific countries, this is the first study to systematically test its invariance across a strategically selected, wide range of cultural contexts and adult age groups simultaneously, moving beyond validation in homogenous, young samples to assess its true global utility.

proposals.title: Development and Psychometric Validation of a Short-Form Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI-SF)
proposals.motivation: Weight stigma is a significant public health issue linked to adverse psychological and physical outcomes. The 35-item Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI) is a validated tool, but its length limits its feasibility in time-sensitive clinical assessments and large-scale epidemiological surveys where participant burden is a critical concern. A brief, psychometrically robust version is needed to facilitate more widespread screening and research on weight stigma exposure.
proposals.hypothesis: It is hypothesized that a short-form WeSEI (WeSEI-SF) can be developed that retains the core factor structure and strong psychometric properties (reliability, validity) of the original full-length scale, while significantly reducing administration time.
proposals.method: Using existing WeSEI validation datasets, we will employ a combination of statistical techniques. First, we will conduct confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) to confirm the original factor structure. Then, we will use item response theory (IRT) analyses, specifically examining item discrimination and information parameters, alongside classical test theory metrics (e.g., item-total correlations, factor loadings) to identify the most informative and non-redundant items. A target of 8-12 items will be selected to create the WeSEI-SF. The short form will then be validated in a new, independent sample for factorial validity (via CFA), internal consistency, test-retest reliability, and convergent validity against established measures of weight stigma, psychological distress, and body image.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide researchers and clinicians with a practical, validated tool for efficiently assessing exposure to weight stigma. It will enable the inclusion of weight stigma measurement in large-scale health surveys and routine clinical screening, thereby expanding the evidence base on its prevalence and impact. The work will also contribute methodologically to the literature on scale shortening in health psychology.
proposals.novelty: While scale shortening is common, this will be the first dedicated effort to create a short-form of the WeSEI, a key tool specifically designed to measure exposure (not internalization) to weight stigma. The method integrates both IRT and classical approaches to optimize item selection for this specific construct, moving beyond simple statistical reduction to ensure clinical and research relevance.

proposals.title: Cultural and Occupational Sources of Variance: A Psychometric Investigation of the Depression Anxiety Stress Scales (DASS) Among Chinese Teachers
proposals.motivation: The Depression Anxiety Stress Scales (DASS) is a widely used tool globally, but pre-2026 studies have indicated weaker validity indices in specific populations, such as Chinese teachers. This is a critical issue as teachers face high occupational stress, and accurate mental health assessment is essential for effective intervention. Understanding whether this measurement issue stems from cultural interpretation of items, unique occupational stressors, or methodological factors is vital for ensuring valid assessment in this high-risk group.
proposals.hypothesis: The weaker validity of the DASS among Chinese teachers is not due to random error but can be attributed to specific cultural constructs (e.g., somatization of distress, stigma-related response bias) and occupation-specific stressors that are not adequately captured by the standard DASS factor structure.
proposals.method: This study will employ a multi-method, cross-sectional design. First, confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) will test the fit of the standard three-factor DASS model in a large sample of Chinese teachers. Second, exploratory structural equation modeling (ESEM) will be used to identify potential culturally or occupationally specific factor structures. Third, the study will integrate multiple sources of validity evidence: convergent validity with measures of burnout and work-related rumination, and discriminant validity using the Marlowe-Crowne Social Desirability Scale to assess stigma-driven response bias. Measurement invariance testing will be conducted across gender and levels of perceived weight stigma (using an adapted Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory concept) to examine if stigma exposure moderates scale performance.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a nuanced psychometric evaluation of a key mental health tool in an understudied occupational and cultural context. It will advance the field by delineating specific cultural and occupational sources of measurement variance, moving beyond simply reporting poor fit. The findings will inform the potential adaptation of the DASS or the development of targeted assessment protocols for Chinese educators.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has noted DASS validity issues in China, this proposal uniquely integrates the researcher's expertise in stigma measurement (WeSEI) and invariance testing to investigate occupational stigma and response bias as explicit methodological factors affecting scale validity in a teacher population, a novel intersection of cultural and occupational psychometrics.

proposals.title: Network Psychometrics of the DASS-21: Identifying Central Symptoms and Bridges Between Depression and Anxiety in a Cross-Cultural Sample
proposals.motivation: The Depression, Anxiety and Stress Scales (DASS-21) is a widely used transdiagnostic measure, yet its latent structure and the relationships between its subscales remain debated. Pre-2026, network psychometrics has emerged as a powerful tool for modeling psychological constructs as systems of interacting symptoms, offering a novel way to understand comorbidity. Applying this method to the DASS-21 can move beyond traditional factor models to identify specific symptoms that are central to the network and may act as bridges between depression and anxiety clusters.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that network analysis will reveal a subset of highly central symptoms within the DASS-21 network and identify specific bridge symptoms (e.g., items related to agitation, anhedonia, or negative affect) that statistically connect the depression and anxiety communities, which may not be apparent from traditional factor analytic studies.
proposals.method: We will employ Gaussian Graphical Model (GGM) network analysis using the `qgraph` package in R. Data will be collected from a large, cross-cultural sample (e.g., UK and Malaysian young adults), leveraging Dr. Griffiths's expertise in cross-cultural psychometrics. The network will be estimated with the Graphical LASSO (GLASSO) algorithm with EBIC model selection to obtain a sparse, interpretable network. Centrality indices (strength, betweenness, closeness) and the bridge strength index will be calculated to identify central and bridge symptoms. Network comparison tests will assess invariance across cultural groups.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, symptom-level map of the DASS-21 structure, highlighting potential intervention targets (central symptoms) and mechanistic pathways (bridge symptoms) for comorbid depression and anxiety. It will extend network psychometrics to a key transdiagnostic instrument and demonstrate the utility of this approach for understanding scale structure beyond traditional factor analysis, with implications for both assessment and targeted therapeutic strategies.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis has been applied to depression and anxiety inventories separately, its application to model the specific structure of the DASS-21 as an integrated system is novel. Furthermore, integrating this with Dr. Griffiths's methodological focus on cross-cultural measurement invariance (as seen in his WeSEI and Nomophobia work) provides a unique contribution by testing the stability of the identified network structure across different populations.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of the DASS-4: An Ultra-Brief Screener for Teacher Mental Health
proposals.motivation: Teacher mental health is a critical public health concern, with high stress, anxiety, and depression levels impacting educator well-being and student outcomes. Pre-2026, there is a recognized need for rapid, low-burden screening tools for use in school settings, but existing full-length scales like the 42-item DASS are impractical for routine monitoring. A validated ultra-brief screener would enable efficient, large-scale assessment to identify at-risk teachers for timely support.
proposals.hypothesis: A 4-item version of the Depression, Anxiety, and Stress Scales (DASS-4) can be developed that maintains strong psychometric properties, including factorial validity, internal consistency, and convergent validity with the full DASS-21, making it a viable ultra-brief screener for teacher populations.
proposals.method: The study will employ a multi-phase, quantitative design. First, items will be selected from the DASS-21 using a combination of statistical (e.g., factor loadings from confirmatory factor analysis, item-total correlations) and content validity approaches to ensure one item represents each core construct: depression, anxiety, stress, and physiological hyperarousal. The selected DASS-4 will then be administered alongside the DASS-21 and a measure of general psychological distress (e.g., Kessler-6) to a large sample of school teachers. Analyses will include confirmatory factor analysis to test the hypothesized one-factor structure, tests of measurement invariance across key demographics (e.g., gender, teaching level), and assessment of reliability and convergent validity.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first validated, domain-specific ultra-brief screener for teacher mental health, directly addressing a gap in practical assessment tools. It will contribute a rigorously tested instrument (the DASS-4) that can be deployed in resource-constrained educational settings for rapid screening and monitoring, facilitating early intervention. The work will also extend the literature on ultra-brief scale development within the specific, high-stress occupational context of teaching.
proposals.novelty: While brief versions of general mental health screeners exist, developing a 4-item screener specifically from the well-validated DASS framework for the teacher population is novel. The approach integrates Dr. Griffiths's recent expertise in scale validation (e.g., WeSEI) and measurement invariance testing across groups into a new domain of occupational mental health.

proposals.title: Psychometric Evaluation and Measurement Invariance of the Malay Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI) in a Nationally Representative Sample of Malaysian Adults
proposals.motivation: Weight stigma is a significant public health issue linked to adverse psychological and physical outcomes. Pre-2026 research by Griffiths and colleagues has validated the WeSEI in student populations, but its generalizability to the broader adult population, which experiences diverse social and occupational contexts, remains unknown. Establishing the instrument's validity in a general adult sample is crucial for accurate epidemiological assessment and intervention planning in Malaysia.
proposals.hypothesis: The Malay WeSEI will demonstrate robust psychometric properties (factorial validity, reliability) in a non-student adult population. Furthermore, measurement invariance will be established across key demographic subgroups (e.g., gender, age, BMI category), indicating that the scale measures the same construct in the same way across these groups.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cross-sectional survey with a stratified random sample of 1500 Malaysian adults (aged 25-60), recruited via a market research panel to ensure demographic representativity. Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA) using maximum likelihood estimation will test the pre-established WeSEI factor structure. Multi-group CFA will assess measurement invariance across gender, age groups, and BMI categories, testing configural, metric, and scalar invariance. Internal consistency will be evaluated using Cronbach's alpha and McDonald's omega. Convergent validity will be assessed via correlations with measures of depression (PHQ-9), anxiety (GAD-7), and self-esteem (Rosenberg Self-Esteem Scale).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first validated tool for assessing weight stigma exposure in the general Malaysian adult population, enabling more accurate prevalence estimates. It will confirm whether findings from student-based research generalize to adults, informing the design of stigma-reduction interventions tailored to diverse adult experiences. The invariance testing will ensure the scale can be used for valid group comparisons in future research.
proposals.novelty: While the WeSEI has been validated in Malaysian students, this is the first study to test its psychometrics and, critically, its measurement invariance in a nationally representative adult sample. This moves beyond convenience sampling to establish the tool's utility for population-level research and policy.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Temporal Stability of the Malay Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI): A Test-Retest Reliability Study Among Malaysian Young Adults
proposals.motivation: Weight stigma is a significant public health issue linked to adverse psychological and physical outcomes. The Malay WeSEI, recently validated by Griffiths and colleagues, provides a crucial tool for assessing exposure to weight stigma in the Malaysian context. However, its psychometric evaluation is incomplete without establishing test-retest reliability, a fundamental property for determining if the instrument yields consistent scores over time, which is essential for its use in longitudinal research and clinical assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: It is hypothesized that the Malay WeSEI will demonstrate good to excellent test-retest reliability, as indicated by intraclass correlation coefficients (ICCs) > 0.75 for its total and subscale scores over a two-week interval. Furthermore, no significant mean differences in scores between the two time points are expected.
proposals.method: A sample of approximately 200 Malaysian young adults will complete the Malay WeSEI at two time points, spaced two weeks apart, a standard interval for assessing temporal stability without expecting substantive change in stigma exposure. The primary analysis will involve calculating two-way mixed-effects, absolute agreement, single-measure intraclass correlation coefficients (ICCs) for the total and subscale scores. Paired-sample t-tests will be used to examine systematic mean differences between Time 1 and Time 2. Internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha) at both time points will also be reported.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence for the temporal stability of the Malay WeSEI, completing its foundational psychometric profile. It will strengthen the instrument's credibility for researchers investigating the prevalence and longitudinal impact of weight stigma in Malaysia. The findings will also inform healthcare professionals about the tool's suitability for monitoring stigma exposure in clinical or intervention settings.
proposals.novelty: While the initial validation of the Malay WeSEI established its factor structure and concurrent validity, its test-retest reliability remains unexamined. This study directly addresses this gap, a critical and non-obvious next step for a newly translated and validated scale, ensuring it meets all standard psychometric criteria for robust measurement.

proposals.title: Pathways to Problematic Social Media Use Beyond ADHD: A Cross-Cultural Study of the Low-ADHD, High-Addiction Profile
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research strongly links ADHD symptoms to social media addiction (SMA), potentially overshadowing alternative etiological pathways. This narrow focus risks mischaracterizing a significant subgroup of individuals with high SMA but low ADHD, for whom different psychological and social factors may be primary drivers. Identifying these distinct pathways is crucial for developing targeted, effective interventions that are not solely based on an ADHD framework.
proposals.hypothesis: Individuals with high levels of social media addiction but low ADHD symptoms will exhibit significantly higher levels of weight stigma exposure, lower general mattering, and greater nomophobia compared to those with the high-ADHD/high-SMA profile, suggesting a distinct psychosocial pathway to addiction.
proposals.method: A cross-sectional, survey-based study will recruit young adults from Indonesia, Malaysia, and the UK. Validated scales will measure SMA (e.g., BSMAS), ADHD symptoms (e.g., ASRS-v1.1), weight stigma exposure (WeSEI), general mattering, and nomophobia (NMP-Q). A latent profile analysis (LPA) will be conducted to identify distinct subgroups based on SMA and ADHD scores. Subsequent ANCOVAs and structural equation modeling (SEM) will test for significant differences in psychosocial variables (WeSEI, mattering, nomophobia) across the derived profiles, controlling for demographics.
proposals.contribution: This study will empirically validate the existence of a clinically relevant, low-ADHD/high-SMA subgroup, challenging the dominant ADHD-centric model of SMA. It will identify specific, alternative risk factors (e.g., weight stigma, low mattering) for this population, informing the development of more nuanced assessment tools and tailored intervention strategies that address stigma and social disconnection.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines ADHD and SMA correlates separately, this proposal directly contrasts psychosocial profiles *across* addiction pathways, using a person-centered (LPA) approach rather than variable-centered correlations. It innovatively applies Dr. Griffiths's expertise in weight stigma (WeSEI) and mattering to explain SMA in a population not primarily driven by impulsivity or attention deficits.

proposals.title: Developmental and Clinical Variations in the Mediation Model of Weight Stigma Exposure: A Multi-Group Study Across Adolescents, Young Adults, Older Adults, and Clinical Populations
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Griffiths' work on the Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI), has established a robust link between weight stigma exposure and adverse psychosocial outcomes, primarily in young adult samples. However, the developmental trajectory of this relationship remains unexplored. It is critical to test whether the established mediation models (e.g., stigma leading to poor mental health via internalized stigma or low mattering) are invariant across key life stages and vulnerable groups, as this has direct implications for targeted intervention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: The strength and significance of the mediation pathways between weight stigma exposure (WeSEI), internalized weight stigma/mattering, and psychological outcomes (e.g., depression, anxiety, self-esteem) will differ significantly across developmental stages (adolescents, young adults, older adults) and between non-clinical and clinical populations (e.g., individuals seeking bariatric surgery or eating disorder treatment).
proposals.method: This cross-sectional study will recruit four distinct samples: adolescents (13-17), young adults (18-25), older adults (65+), and a clinical sample with weight-related concerns. All participants will complete the WeSEI, measures of internalized weight stigma, mattering, and psychological outcomes. Using structural equation modeling (SEM), a baseline mediation model will be established. Multi-group confirmatory factor analysis (MG-CFA) will first test for measurement invariance of the WeSEI across groups. Subsequently, multi-group SEM will be employed to statistically compare the structural path coefficients of the mediation model across the four groups, identifying where significant differences exist.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical test of the generalizability of core weight stigma models beyond the standard young adult sample. It will identify developmental periods of heightened vulnerability or resilience, informing life-stage-specific anti-stigma programs. Furthermore, validating the WeSEI and model in clinical populations will enhance its utility in healthcare assessment and treatment planning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Griffiths and others has validated the WeSEI across cultures and genders, no study has systematically examined how the entire psychological process of weight stigma differs from adolescence to late adulthood and in clinical settings, moving beyond simple mean-level comparisons to test the invariance of structural relationships.

proposals.title: Cross-Cultural Validation of the Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI): A Multi-National Study Testing Measurement Invariance and Associations with Psychological Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Weight stigma is a global public health concern, but its measurement and impact are heavily influenced by cultural norms and values. Pre-2026 research has validated the WeSEI in specific populations (e.g., US, Malaysia, Indonesia), but its structural validity and generalizability across diverse, non-Western and non-Chinese cultural contexts remain untested. Establishing a culturally robust measure is critical for accurate cross-cultural comparisons and for developing effective, context-sensitive interventions to mitigate the harmful effects of weight stigma.
proposals.hypothesis: The factor structure of the WeSEI will demonstrate configural and metric invariance across distinct cultural groups, indicating that the construct of weight stigma exposure is conceptualized similarly. Furthermore, the strength of associations between WeSEI scores and psychological outcomes (e.g., depression, self-esteem) will vary significantly across cultures, moderated by cultural values such as collectivism/individualism.
proposals.method: This study will employ a cross-sectional, survey-based design. We will recruit large, community-based samples of adults from three distinct cultural regions (e.g., the Arab Gulf states, Sub-Saharan Africa, and Scandinavia). Participants will complete the WeSEI, measures of psychological distress, and a cultural values scale. Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA) will be used to test the pre-established WeSEI factor structure in each sample. Multi-group CFA will then be conducted to formally test for measurement invariance across the cultural groups. Finally, multi-group structural equation modeling (SEM) will examine and compare the paths from WeSEI scores to psychological outcomes across groups.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first rigorous test of the WeSEI's cross-cultural applicability, determining if it is a truly universal tool or requires cultural adaptation. It will advance the field by mapping how the psychological consequences of weight stigma exposure vary across cultural contexts, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all understanding. The findings will inform the development of culturally tailored public health strategies to address weight-based discrimination.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has translated the WeSEI, this proposal systematically tests its measurement invariance across maximally dissimilar cultures, a critical step not yet undertaken. It uniquely integrates cultural value theory as a moderator, moving beyond simple validation to explain *why* associations between stigma and outcomes might differ globally.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Validation of the Adolescent Cyber-Social Identity Development Scale (ACSID-11): Predictive Validity for Psychosocial Outcomes and Stability Across Time
proposals.motivation: The Adolescent Cyber-Social Identity Development Scale (ACSID-11) is a recently developed tool for assessing identity development processes in online contexts. As digital interactions become central to adolescent development, validated measures are crucial. However, its psychometric properties have only been examined cross-sectionally, leaving its predictive validity and temporal stability unknown, which are essential for its use in longitudinal research and clinical assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: The ACSID-11 will demonstrate strong test-retest reliability and measurement invariance over a 12-month period. Furthermore, baseline ACSID-11 scores will significantly predict subsequent levels of psychosocial well-being, problematic smartphone use, and experiences of online social stigma.
proposals.method: A three-wave longitudinal panel study will be conducted over 12 months (baseline, 6-month, 12-month follow-up) with a multi-national sample of adolescents (N=800, aged 13-18). Using confirmatory factor analysis (CFA), we will test for longitudinal measurement invariance across the three time points. Predictive validity will be assessed via cross-lagged panel models (CLPM) and latent growth curve modeling (LGCM), regressing follow-up scores on validated measures of self-esteem, depression, the Smartphone Application-Based Addiction Scale, and the Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI) onto baseline ACSID-11 factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal evidence for the stability and predictive utility of the ACSID-11, solidifying its role as a key tool for developmental cyber-psychology. It will elucidate how early online identity development processes shape later psychosocial outcomes, informing targeted intervention programs.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by this researcher has validated stigma and nomophobia measures cross-sectionally and across groups, this proposal applies a rigorous longitudinal validation framework to a novel identity development scale, moving beyond static assessment to model dynamic predictive relationships over time.

proposals.title: Cross-Cultural Validation of the Appearance-Contingent Self-Worth Inventory (ACSID-11): A Multi-National Study Testing Measurement Invariance and Associations with Weight Stigma Exposure
proposals.motivation: The ACSID-11 is a key tool for assessing self-worth contingent on appearance, a construct linked to negative mental health outcomes. Its use in cross-cultural research and global mental health surveillance is limited without robust evidence that it measures the same construct equivalently across diverse populations. Establishing measurement invariance is a prerequisite for valid cross-group comparisons, especially in the context of weight stigma, which varies culturally.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the ACSID-11 will demonstrate configural and metric, but not scalar, invariance across samples from at least three distinct cultural/national groups (e.g., UK, Malaysia, Indonesia). We further hypothesize that the strength of the association between Weight Stigma Exposure (WeSEI) and ACSID-11 scores will be invariant across these groups.
proposals.method: We will recruit large, independent samples of young adults from the UK, Malaysia, and Indonesia (N ≈ 500 per group). Participants will complete the ACSID-11, the Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI), and demographic measures. Using confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) in Mplus or R (lavaan), we will test a multi-group measurement invariance hierarchy: configural, metric, and scalar. The DIFFTEST procedure will be used for nested model comparisons. Following invariance testing, a multi-group structural equation model will test the invariance of the regression path from WeSEI to ACSID-11 latent factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first rigorous test of the ACSID-11's cross-cultural validity, clarifying its suitability for international research. It will directly inform best practices for comparing appearance-contingent self-worth across cultures. Furthermore, by integrating the WeSEI, it will advance understanding of how weight stigma relates to self-worth mechanisms in diverse societal contexts.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Griffiths' team has validated the WeSEI cross-culturally and tested invariance for other tools, no study has applied this rigorous multi-group CFA framework to the ACSID-11 across nations. The novel integration of weight stigma exposure as an invariant external correlate strengthens the validation beyond simple factor structure testing.

proposals.title: Network Structure and Centrality of Problematic Use of the Internet (PUI) Criteria: A Cross-Cultural Study Using Network Psychometrics
proposals.motivation: Problematic Use of the Internet (PUI) is a growing public health concern, yet its symptom structure is often treated as a unidimensional latent factor. This approach may obscure the complex, dynamic interactions between specific behavioral criteria (e.g., preoccupation, loss of control, mood modification). Understanding these symptom-symptom interactions is crucial for developing targeted interventions. Pre-2026, network analysis has been successfully applied to other psychopathologies (e.g., depression, PTSD) but remains underutilized in PUI research, especially across diverse populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the network structure of PUI criteria will reveal distinct clusters (e.g., cognitive preoccupation vs. behavioral dyscontrol symptoms) and that specific symptoms (e.g., 'withdrawal' or 'continued use despite harm') will act as central bridges connecting these clusters, potentially serving as key intervention targets. Furthermore, we hypothesize this network structure will demonstrate measurement invariance across different PUI types (e.g., social media vs. gaming) and cultural groups.
proposals.method: We will recruit large, cross-cultural samples (e.g., from the UK, Malaysia, and Indonesia) and assess PUI using established scales (e.g., the Internet Addiction Test). Data will be analyzed using Gaussian Graphical Model (GGM) network estimation with graphical LASSO regularization and the Extended Bayesian Information Criterion (EBIC) for sparsity. We will compute node centrality indices (strength, betweenness, closeness) to identify key symptoms.
