Network comparison tests (NCT) will be used to assess invariance across PUI types (e.g., social media vs. gaming) and cultural groups, building on Dr. Griffiths' prior invariance work.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed network model of PUI symptom interactions across cultures and specific internet applications. It will move the field beyond latent factor models to a dynamic system view, identifying potential causal maintenance factors and high-leverage points for clinical intervention. The findings will also inform the refinement of diagnostic criteria by highlighting the most influential and bridge symptoms within the PUI construct.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis has been applied in psychopathology, its application to PUI is novel, particularly in modeling interactions *across* specific internet use types (e.g., gaming, social media) and cultural contexts. This integrates Dr. Griffiths' expertise in psychometric invariance testing with cutting-edge network psychometrics to deconstruct PUI's architecture.

proposals.title: Psychometric Evaluation and Validation of the Adult and Older Adult Compulsive Social Media Use Disorder Scale (A-OA-ACSID-11): Measurement Invariance Across Age Cohorts and Associations with Well-being
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, research on compulsive social media use (CSMU) has heavily focused on adolescent and young adult populations, using scales like the ACSID-11. However, the proliferation of social media use among middle-aged and older adults, a trend well-documented pre-2026, necessitates validated assessment tools for these growing at-risk groups. The lack of age-appropriate, psychometrically sound instruments hinders the accurate identification of CSMU and its correlates, such as loneliness and depression, in adult and elderly populations.
proposals.hypothesis: The ACSID-11 can be successfully adapted and validated for use with adult (ages 30-64) and older adult (ages 65+) populations, demonstrating strong factorial validity, reliability, and measurement invariance across these age cohorts. Furthermore, scores on the adapted scale will show significant positive correlations with established measures of psychological distress (e.g., depression, anxiety) and negative correlations with well-being indicators.
proposals.method: The study will employ a cross-sectional, survey-based design with separate samples of adults (n=500) and older adults (n=500). The method will involve a multi-step psychometric evaluation: 1) Expert review and cognitive interviews to adapt ACSID-11 items for linguistic and contextual relevance to older populations. 2) Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA) using robust maximum likelihood estimation to test the original one-factor structure of the ACSID-11 in each sample. 3) Multi-group CFA to establish measurement invariance (configural, metric, scalar) across the two age cohorts. 4) Assessment of convergent and discriminant validity by correlating scores with measures of nomophobia, depression (PHQ-9), loneliness (UCLA Loneliness Scale), and a social desirability scale.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first validated tool specifically adapted for assessing compulsive social media use in adult and older adult populations, filling a critical gap in the behavioral addiction literature. It will establish crucial normative data and invariance properties, enabling accurate cross-age group comparisons and tailored clinical screening. The findings will inform the development of targeted prevention and intervention strategies for these understudied demographics.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Griffiths's recent work has validated stigma scales like the WeSEI across cultures and genders, this proposal applies his psychometric expertise to the novel domain of gerontological behavioral addiction, adapting a CSMU scale for older adults—a population largely overlooked in pre-2026 digital addiction research. The explicit test of measurement invariance between middle-aged and older adults is a non-obvious and methodologically rigorous extension of his invariance-testing approach.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Digital Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) Intervention to Reduce Stress and Improve Preventive Health Behaviors Among Informal Caregivers: The Role of Weight Stigma Exposure and Mattering
proposals.motivation: Informal caregivers experience high levels of chronic stress, which is a known barrier to engaging in their own preventive health behaviors (e.g., regular exercise, health screenings). Pre-2026 research by Griffiths and colleagues has established weight stigma as a significant psychosocial stressor, measured by the Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI), and mattering as a key psychological resource. The unique stress burden on caregivers, potentially compounded by weight stigma, remains an underexplored pathway to poor self-care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a digital ACT intervention will significantly reduce caregiver stress and improve preventive health behaviors, and that these effects will be mediated by reductions in perceived weight stigma exposure and increases in feelings of mattering.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-arm RCT with 300 informal caregivers. The intervention group will receive a 6-week, self-guided digital ACT program (featuring mindfulness, values clarification, and committed action exercises), while the control group will receive stress-management education pamphlets. Pre-2026 validated scales will be used: the Perceived Stress Scale (PSS), the Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI), the General Mattering Scale (GMS), and a Preventive Health Behaviors Checklist. Data will be analyzed using a 2 (group) x 3 (time: pre, post, 3-month follow-up) mixed ANOVA. Parallel mediation models (using PROCESS Macro v4.0) will test if changes in WeSEI and GMS scores mediate the intervention's effect on stress and health behaviors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence for the efficacy of a scalable, digital ACT intervention tailored for caregivers. It will advance the field by testing a novel theoretical model linking a specific intervention to health outcomes via mechanisms of weight stigma and mattering, directly extending Griffiths' psychometric work on the WeSEI into an interventional context.
proposals.novelty: While ACT and caregiver interventions exist, applying them specifically to improve caregivers' *own* preventive health via the mechanisms of weight stigma exposure and mattering is novel. It integrates Griffiths' expertise in stigma measurement with intervention science to target a high-risk, overlooked population.

proposals.title: Psychometric Evaluation and Measurement Invariance of the Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI) Across Patients with Neurological Conditions and Their Caregivers
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Griffiths and colleagues established the WeSEI as a valid tool for assessing weight stigma exposure. However, its application has been limited to general and young adult populations. Weight stigma is a significant psychosocial stressor that may be uniquely experienced by individuals with chronic neurological conditions like dementia or Parkinson's disease, and their caregivers, potentially exacerbating health disparities and caregiving burden. Investigating this in diverse clinical populations is a critical next step.
proposals.hypothesis: The WeSEI will demonstrate strong psychometric properties (factorial validity, reliability) and measurement invariance across groups defined by neurological condition (dementia vs. Parkinson's disease), patient vs. caregiver status, and gender, indicating it is a robust tool for cross-group comparisons in clinical settings.
proposals.method: This cross-sectional study will recruit dyads of patients (diagnosed with dementia or Parkinson's disease) and their primary caregivers. All participants will complete the WeSEI and measures of related constructs (e.g., depression, quality of life) for validation. We will employ Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA) to test the pre-established WeSEI factor structure. Multi-group CFA will be used to rigorously assess measurement invariance across the key groups (condition, role, gender). Additional analyses will examine differences in WeSEI scores and their correlates.
proposals.contribution: This study will extend the validation of the WeSEI to important and understudied clinical populations, providing a tool to quantify a key social determinant of health in neurology. It will offer the first comparative data on weight stigma exposure across different neurological conditions and between patients and caregivers, informing targeted psychosocial interventions.
proposals.novelty: While the WeSEI has been validated in Malaysia and Indonesia, this is the first proposal to apply it to the context of progressive neurological disorders and to explicitly test invariance between patients and their caregivers, a critical dyadic relationship in chronic disease management.

proposals.title: Tailoring Health Messaging for Weight Stigma: A Psychometric Study of Fear and Stress as Moderators of Message Efficacy
proposals.motivation: Weight stigma is a pervasive public health issue linked to adverse psychological and physical outcomes. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Griffiths's work on the Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI), has established its prevalence and impact. However, generic anti-stigma campaigns may be ineffective or even harmful if they fail to account for individual differences in emotional vulnerability, such as elevated fear and stress, which are common consequences of stigma exposure.
proposals.hypothesis: Health communication messages designed to reduce weight stigma will demonstrate greater efficacy (e.g., improved attitudes, reduced bias) when they are tailored to an individual's baseline levels of fear and stress related to weight, compared to standardized, non-tailored messaging.
proposals.method: This study will employ a quantitative, experimental psychometric design. First, a large sample will complete validated measures of weight stigma exposure (WeSEI), weight bias internalization, and specific scales for weight-related fear and stress. Using exploratory and confirmatory factor analysis, we will identify distinct fear and stress profiles. Subsequently, participants will be randomized to view health messages (varying in framing, e.g., empathy-inducing vs. factual) tailored to their profile or a control message. Efficacy will be assessed via pre-post changes in explicit and implicit weight bias measures, with analyses (e.g., MANCOVA) testing for moderation by the fear/stress factors.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first empirical framework for tailoring anti-weight-stigma communications based on pre-existing emotional states. It will extend the application of the WeSEI from assessment to intervention design. The findings will offer concrete, evidence-based guidance for public health campaigns and clinical communications to maximize their positive impact and minimize unintended harm.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has measured stigma exposure and its correlates, the novel integration of psychometrically-derived fear/stress profiles to actively tailor and test the efficacy of health messaging represents a significant advance from descriptive research to targeted intervention development.

proposals.title: A Network Psychometric Analysis of Weight Stigma, Fear of Missing Out, and Problematic Social Media Use Among Young Adults
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Griffiths and others has established weight stigma (WeSEI) and nomophobia as significant psychosocial stressors, particularly among young adults. However, the specific interconnections between these constructs—such as weight stigma exposure, fear of missing out (FoMO), perceived stress, and susceptibility to social comparison—remain poorly understood as a dynamic system. Understanding this network is crucial for developing targeted interventions that address the core drivers of psychological distress in the age of pervasive social media.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a network analysis will reveal that perceived stress and susceptibility to social comparison act as key bridge symptoms, directly connecting exposure to weight stigma (WeSEI) with fear of missing out and subsequent problematic social media use behaviors.
proposals.method: We will recruit a large, cross-cultural sample of young adults (e.g., from the UK and Malaysia) and administer validated scales: the Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI), a nomophobia measure, the Fear of Missing Out scale, the Perceived Stress Scale, and a social comparison susceptibility measure. Using R packages `qgraph` and `bootnet`, we will estimate a Gaussian Graphical Model (GGM) to visualize the partial correlation network. We will compute centrality indices (strength, betweenness) to identify the most influential nodes and perform the Network Comparison Test (NCT) to examine structural invariance across groups (e.g., gender, levels of problematic smartphone use).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first network model elucidating the direct and indirect pathways linking weight stigma to digital behavior problems. It will move beyond traditional factor-analytic approaches to identify potential intervention targets (high-centrality symptoms) within the psychological system. The cross-cultural invariance testing will clarify whether these network dynamics are universal or context-dependent.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by this team has validated the WeSEI and examined measurement invariance, applying network psychometrics to model the causal interplay between weight stigma and technology-related fears represents a significant methodological and conceptual advance. It shifts the focus from latent factors to active symptom-level interactions within this specific psychosocial domain.

proposals.title: Inferring Weight-Related Stress and Fear: A Psychometric and Network Analysis of Preventive Behaviors in Patients and Caregivers
proposals.motivation: Weight stigma is a significant psychosocial stressor linked to poor health outcomes. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Griffiths's work on the WeSEI, focuses on measuring exposure to stigma, but there is a gap in tools that infer the internalized psychological impact (stress, fear) from observable behavioral responses. This 'inverse problem' is crucial for early, non-invasive identification of distress in clinical and community settings.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific patterns of preventive health behaviors (e.g., avoidance of medical appointments, specific dietary restrictions, exercise routines) can serve as reliable, quantifiable indicators of underlying weight-related stress and fear in both individuals with high weight and their caregivers.
proposals.method: We will develop and validate a new 'Preventive Behavior Inventory' (PBI) through a multi-phase, cross-cultural study. Following established scale development protocols from the WeSEI papers, we will use exploratory and confirmatory factor analysis (EFA/CFA) to identify behavioral subscales. Subsequently, we will employ network analysis (using Gaussian Graphical Models) to map the probabilistic relationships between specific behavioral nodes and latent constructs of stress and fear, measured via established scales (e.g., Perceived Stress Scale, Fear of Fat scale). Measurement invariance testing across groups (patient vs. caregiver, gender, culture) will be conducted.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a novel, behaviorally-anchored tool for indirectly assessing weight-related distress, complementing existing exposure measures like the WeSEI. It will advance the theoretical understanding of how stigma is internalized and manifested, offering clinicians a practical method for identifying at-risk individuals based on observable actions rather than self-reported affect alone.
proposals.novelty: While prior work measures stigma exposure or direct psychological states, this study innovatively treats observable behaviors as the *input* to statistically infer hidden emotional states, applying a psychometric 'inverse problem' framework to the domain of weight stigma for the first time.

proposals.title: A Longitudinal and Psychometric Test of the I-PACE Model: Refining the ICOPES-TW to Assess Weight Stigma Pathways
proposals.motivation: The Interaction of Person-Affect-Cognition-Execution (I-PACE) model provides a dominant theoretical framework for understanding the development and maintenance of addictive behaviors, including problematic technology use. However, its longitudinal pathways, particularly concerning psychosocial stressors like weight stigma, remain under-tested. Concurrently, the ICOPES-TW (Inventory of Cognitions and Perceptions Experienced in Stigma - Target Weight) instrument, designed to measure core cognitive-affective responses to weight stigma, requires longitudinal validation to ensure its scales are sensitive to change and predictive of outcomes over time.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that longitudinal data will support the core pathways of the I-PACE model, where exposure to weight stigma (WeSEI) predicts subsequent maladaptive cognitive-affective responses (ICOPES-TW), which in turn predict increased addictive or compensatory behaviors. Furthermore, we hypothesize that iterative refinement of the ICOPES-TW based on longitudinal data will significantly improve its factorial validity and predictive utility within the model.
proposals.method: We will conduct a three-wave longitudinal study over 12 months with a large sample of young adults, administering the WeSEI, ICOPES-TW, and measures of affective dysregulation and addictive behaviors (e.g., problematic smartphone use). We will employ cross-lagged panel modeling (CLPM) to test I-PACE pathways. Concurrently, we will perform iterative psychometric refinement: after each wave, we will use exploratory and confirmatory factor analysis (EFA/CFA) on split samples to assess the ICOPES-TW's structure, employing item response theory (IRT) and reliability analysis to identify and revise weak items. The refined instrument will be used in subsequent waves, creating a feedback loop.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a rare longitudinal test of the I-PACE model in the context of weight stigma, moving beyond cross-sectional correlation. It will yield a psychometrically robust, longitudinally validated version of the ICOPES-TW, enhancing measurement precision for future research. The synergistic approach demonstrates how theory testing and measure development can be integrated to advance both fields.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of a longitudinal causal model test with an iterative, data-driven psychometric refinement process within the same study. This creates a dynamic feedback loop where model testing improves the measure, and the improved measure, in turn, refines the model test, a methodology not commonly applied in addiction or stigma research pre-2026.

proposals.title: Bidirectional Causal Pathways Between Wearable-Related Self-Surveillance and Psychological Distress: A Cross-Lagged Panel Study in Clinical Weight Management and Diverse Community Cohorts
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of health wearables has introduced new sources of self-surveillance and potential stress, particularly for individuals in weight management contexts who may already experience weight stigma. Pre-2026 research has established links between technology use and distress, but the causal direction and its moderators remain unclear. This gap is critical for developing ethical guidelines and interventions, especially for clinical populations where wearables are often prescribed.
proposals.hypothesis: The dominant causal direction between Wearable-Related Self-Surveillance (WRSS) and psychological distress differs based on clinical status and sociocultural context, with a stronger distress-to-use pathway in clinical weight management groups and a more bidirectional or use-to-distress pathway in general community cohorts.
proposals.method: A longitudinal design with three waves (baseline, 6-month, 12-month) will be employed across two distinct samples: a clinical weight management group and a diverse community sample stratified by age and cultural background (e.g., UK, Malaysia). WRSS will be measured using a newly developed and validated scale based on pre-2026 concepts of problematic use and self-surveillance. Psychological distress will be assessed via established measures (e.g., DASS-21). Cross-lagged panel models (CLPM) will be the primary analytical technique to test bidirectional causality, with multi-group invariance testing (using confirmatory factor analysis and nested model comparisons) to examine differences across clinical status and cultural groups.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical test of bidirectional causality in the wearable-distress relationship, moving beyond correlational evidence. It will identify for whom these potentially harmful cycles are most pronounced, directly informing tailored clinical recommendations for wearable use. The findings will also extend the application of stigma and 'mattering' frameworks into the novel context of digital self-tracking.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Griffiths and others has validated stigma exposure tools (WeSEI) and examined psychometrics across groups, applying a longitudinal, causal methodology to the novel construct of Wearable-Related Self-Surveillance in a direct clinical-comparison design is unprecedented. It fuses methodological expertise in invariance testing with the substantive study of how technological stressors interact with pre-existing clinical vulnerabilities.

proposals.title: Cross-Cultural and Clinical Validation of the Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI): Measurement Invariance and Associations with Health Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Griffiths and others has established the WeSEI as a critical tool for assessing exposure to weight stigma in general and student populations. However, its psychometric properties and applicability in high-stakes clinical settings (e.g., bariatric surgery candidates) and across broader cultural and age demographics remain unvalidated. This gap limits understanding of how weight stigma functions in populations most affected by it and hinders comparative global research.
proposals.hypothesis: The WeSEI will demonstrate configural, metric, and scalar measurement invariance across clinical (weight management) and non-clinical groups, as well as across distinct cultural samples (e.g., UK, Southeast Asia, Middle East). Furthermore, WeSEI scores will show stronger positive associations with psychological distress and poorer health behaviors in clinical populations compared to general population groups.
proposals.method: This multi-site study will employ confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) to test the established WeSEI factor structure in three new cohorts: a UK clinical weight management group, a Malaysian older adult community sample, and a Turkish adult general population sample. Using Mplus software, multi-group CFA will be conducted to test for measurement invariance across these groups and against existing validation samples. Hierarchical regression analyses will then examine if group status (clinical vs. non-clinical) moderates the relationship between WeSEI scores and validated outcomes (e.g., depression, anxiety, quality of life, internalized stigma).
proposals.contribution: This research will provide essential evidence for the validity of the WeSEI in clinical and diverse cultural contexts, enabling its use in intervention studies and global health disparities research. It will elucidate whether the experience and impact of weight stigma exposure is quantitatively and qualitatively different for individuals actively seeking medical weight management, informing more tailored clinical support.
proposals.novelty: While prior work validated the WeSEI in young adults and general populations, this is the first proposed study to rigorously test its utility and measurement properties in clinical weight management settings and to concurrently establish cross-cultural invariance beyond initial Malay translations, addressing a key limitation in the field's measurement tools.

proposals.title: National Prevalence and Correlates of Weight Stigma Exposure: A Psychometric Evaluation of the Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI) in a Representative Adult Population
proposals.motivation: Weight stigma is a pervasive public health issue linked to adverse psychological and physical outcomes. Pre-2026 research has validated the WeSEI in specific, non-representative groups (e.g., college students, young adults), but the true population prevalence and demographic patterning of weight stigma exposure remain unknown. A nationally representative assessment is critical to inform evidence-based public health interventions and policy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the WeSEI will demonstrate strong psychometric properties (factorial validity, reliability, measurement invariance) in a large, nationally representative adult sample, and that exposure levels will vary significantly across demographic subgroups (e.g., age, gender, BMI category, socioeconomic status).
proposals.method: We will administer the WeSEI, alongside measures of psychological well-being (e.g., depression, anxiety, self-esteem) and demographic/health variables, within a large-scale, cross-sectional, nationally representative survey (e.g., integrated into an existing national panel). Psychometric evaluation will employ confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) to test the established WeSEI factor structure. Multi-group CFA will test for measurement invariance across key demographic groups. Prevalence estimates and subgroup differences will be analyzed using complex survey-weighted regression models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first nationally representative benchmark data on weight stigma exposure, quantifying its public health burden. It will robustly validate the WeSEI for population-level research and identify high-risk subgroups, offering a crucial evidence base for targeted stigma-reduction initiatives.
proposals.novelty: While prior WeSEI validation studies used convenience samples, this is the first to deploy the scale in a rigorous, nationally representative design across the full adult age range, moving from instrument validation to definitive population science.

proposals.title: Protective Factors Against Problematic Internet Use: A Cross-Cultural Psychometric Study of Resilience, Mattering, and Healthy Digital Habits
proposals.motivation: Research on Problematic Internet Use (PIU) predominantly focuses on risk factors and psychopathology, creating a deficit-focused literature. A critical gap exists in understanding the psychological and behavioral factors that protect individuals from developing PIU, which is essential for developing balanced, strength-based interventions. This study addresses this gap by investigating protective factors, aligning with a growing pre-2026 interest in positive psychology within behavioral addictions.
proposals.hypothesis: It is hypothesized that higher levels of psychological resilience, sense of mattering, and engagement in structured, healthy internet use activities will significantly predict lower scores on the ACSID-11, indicating a protective effect against PIU. This relationship is expected to be invariant across different cultural groups.
proposals.method: A cross-sectional, multi-national survey will be administered to young adults in the UK, Malaysia, and Indonesia. Validated scales will measure PIU (ACSID-11), resilience (Connor-Davidson Resilience Scale), mattering (General Mattering Scale), and healthy internet use (a newly developed scale assessing purposeful online activities). Data analysis will employ confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) to validate the healthy internet use construct, followed by structural equation modeling (SEM) to test the predictive model of protective factors on ACSID-11 scores. Measurement invariance testing (configural, metric, scalar) will be conducted across the three national samples to ensure cross-cultural validity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated model of psychological and behavioral protective factors against PIU, validated across cultures. It will contribute a novel psychometric tool for assessing healthy internet use and shift the field's focus towards prevention and resilience. The findings will directly inform the development of positive psychology interventions aimed at fostering digital well-being.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Griffiths and colleagues has validated stigma and nomophobia measures across cultures, this proposal uniquely applies invariance testing methodologies to a model of protective factors. It moves beyond assessing pathology to empirically identify sources of digital resilience, a non-obvious shift given the field's historical emphasis on addiction correlates.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Cognitive Dissonance-Based Intervention to Reduce Weight Bias Internalization and Activity Avoidance Among Young Adults with Overweight and Obesity
proposals.motivation: Weight stigma exposure is a pervasive public health issue linked to psychological harm and health-compromising behaviors, including avoidance of physical activity. Pre-2026 research, including the development of the Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI), has established the role of weight bias internalization (WBI) as a critical mediator of these negative outcomes. However, there is a significant lack of evidence-based interventions specifically designed to reduce WBI and its behavioral consequences, such as activity avoidance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a brief, online cognitive dissonance intervention, which challenges internalized weight-biased beliefs, will lead to significant reductions in WBI and, subsequently, self-reported intention to avoid and actual avoidance of health-promoting activities (e.g., gym attendance, group sports) compared to a waitlist control group.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-arm, parallel-group RCT. Participants (N=200 young adults with overweight/obesity and elevated WBI) will be randomized to intervention or waitlist control. The intervention, adapted from successful body image programs, will involve three weekly online modules employing cognitive dissonance techniques (e.g., writing counter-attitudinal essays, challenging stereotype-consistent beliefs). Primary outcomes are changes in WBI (using the Weight Bias Internalization Scale) and activity avoidance (using a validated scale and behavioral log). We will employ confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) to validate measurement models and use structural equation modeling (SEM) with bootstrapping to test the mediation model where reduced WBI leads to reduced activity avoidance.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical test of a targeted intervention for WBI derived from cognitive dissonance theory. It will advance the field by moving beyond measurement (as in the WeSEI validation studies) to experimental intervention, directly addressing a key mechanism linking stigma to health behavior. The findings will offer a scalable, evidence-based tool for clinicians and public health practitioners to mitigate the harmful effects of weight stigma.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by this researcher and others has focused on assessing exposure and internalization, this proposal innovatively applies an established psychological intervention paradigm (cognitive dissonance) to the novel target of WBI and directly tests its impact on a specific, functionally impairing downstream behavior (activity avoidance), which has not been systematically attempted in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Cultural Moderation of the Mediating Role of Weight Stigma Exposure in the Relationship Between Body Mass Index and Psychological Distress: A Cross-Cultural Comparison Between Western and East Asian Samples
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Griffiths's development of the Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI), has established weight stigma as a significant psychosocial stressor linked to adverse mental health outcomes. However, the mechanisms through which stigma operates may vary across cultures with differing norms regarding body weight and self-concept. A cross-cultural test of the mediation model is crucial to determine the universality or cultural specificity of these pathways, informing more culturally attuned interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: The mediating effect of weight stigma exposure (as measured by the WeSEI) on the relationship between Body Mass Index (BMI) and psychological distress (e.g., depression, anxiety) will be significantly stronger in individualistic Western cultures (e.g., UK, US) compared to collectivistic East Asian cultures (e.g., Japan, South Korea), where other factors like social harmony may moderate the stigma experience.
proposals.method: This study will employ a cross-sectional, multi-group design. We will recruit large, matched samples of adults from a Western country (UK) and two East Asian countries (Japan, South Korea). All participants will complete the validated WeSEI, a measure of psychological distress (e.g., DASS-21), and provide self-reported height/weight. The primary analysis will use structural equation modeling (SEM) with multi-group confirmatory factor analysis (MG-CFA) to establish measurement invariance of the WeSEI across groups. Subsequently, a multi-group mediation analysis using the bootstrap method (e.g., PROCESS macro or SEM-based) will test and compare the indirect effect (BMI → WeSEI → Distress) across the cultural groups, formally testing for moderation by culture.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first direct, quantitative comparison of a core psychological model of weight stigma across fundamentally different cultural contexts. It will clarify whether the established Western model is generalizable or requires cultural modification. The findings will critically advance the theoretical understanding of weight stigma as a culturally embedded phenomenon and offer practical guidance for developing public health strategies that are sensitive to cultural norms.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Griffiths and colleagues has validated the WeSEI *within* specific cultures (Indonesia, Malaysia), this proposal moves beyond validation to explicitly test a theoretical mediation model *across* cultures, using rigorous measurement invariance testing and multi-group SEM to isolate cultural moderation—a direct and novel extension of the foundational WeSEI studies.

proposals.title: Network Analysis of Distress and Avoidance Symptoms in Weight Stigma: A Cross-Cultural Psychometric Study
proposals.motivation: Weight stigma is a pervasive stressor linked to significant psychological distress and behavioral avoidance, which can exacerbate health disparities. Pre-2026 research, including Griffiths' development of the Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI), has established the prevalence of these experiences but has largely relied on latent variable models (e.g., factor analysis) that treat symptoms as interchangeable indicators of a common cause. A network approach is needed to model the dynamic, potentially causal, relationships between specific symptoms of distress (e.g., shame, anxiety) and avoidance behaviors (e.g., social withdrawal, exercise avoidance) to identify precise intervention targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that network analysis will reveal a core cluster of symptoms (e.g., internalized shame and anticipatory anxiety) that act as central bridges, strongly connecting experiences of weight stigma exposure to subsequent behavioral avoidance across different cultural groups.
proposals.method: We will recruit samples from the UK, Malaysia, and Indonesia, administering the WeSEI alongside validated measures of psychological distress (e.g., DASS-21) and activity avoidance. Using R packages `qgraph` and `bootnet`, we will estimate regularized partial correlation networks (Gaussian Graphical Models) to visualize symptom relationships. We will compute centrality indices (strength, betweenness, expected influence) to identify key bridge symptoms and conduct the Network Comparison Test to examine measurement invariance of the network structure across cultures and levels of stigma exposure.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first network model of psychological sequelae specific to weight stigma, moving beyond comorbidity rates to map functional symptom connections. It will offer empirically identified, high-leverage targets for clinical intervention (e.g., targeting shame to reduce avoidance) and validate the cross-cultural applicability of network psychometrics in stigma research.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis has been applied to general psychopathology, its application to model the specific mechanistic links between stigma exposure, distinct distress symptoms, and behavioral avoidance is novel. Furthermore, integrating this method with a culturally validated instrument (WeSEI) to test network invariance represents a significant methodological advance in the field.

proposals.title: A Bifactor and Network Analysis of the WHO's Intrinsic Capacity and Functional Ability Constructs: Testing for Empirical Distinctiveness
proposals.motivation: The WHO's Integrated Care for Older People (ICOPE) framework posits intrinsic capacity (IC) and functional ability (FA) as distinct but related constructs central to healthy aging. However, their empirical separation remains a theoretical assumption, with significant measurement overlap suspected. Clarifying this distinction is critical for developing precise, non-stigmatizing assessment tools that accurately target biological capacity versus environmentally influenced function, avoiding the conflation seen in some weight-related health measures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that IC and FA will not emerge as empirically distinct latent constructs in advanced psychometric models, but will instead form a highly interconnected network where specific IC domains (e.g., cognition, locomotion) directly and strongly predict specific FA domains (e.g., mobility, self-care), challenging their theoretical separation.
proposals.method: We will recruit a large, cross-national sample of adults aged 50+ and administer validated scales for all IC domains (vitality, sensory, cognitive, psychological, locomotor) and FA. Using confirmatory factor analysis, we will test a correlated two-factor (IC vs. FA) model against a bifactor model with a general 'health and functioning' factor. Subsequently, we will employ Gaussian Graphical Model (GGM) network analysis to visualize the unique predictive pathways between individual IC and FA indicators, controlling for covariates like age, gender, and weight stigma exposure (using the WeSEI). Measurement invariance testing across gender and nationality will assess scale stability.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first rigorous empirical test of the IC-FA distinction, offering evidence to refine the ICOPE framework. It will advance assessment methodology in gerontology by demonstrating the utility of network models over traditional latent variable models for complex health constructs. The findings will inform more targeted interventions by identifying which specific capacities most directly influence specific functions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has validated IC and FA separately, this is the first to directly model their latent and network-level relationships to test for distinctiveness. Applying bifactor and network analysis—methods used in Dr. Griffiths's prior psychometric work on nomophobia and weight stigma—to this gerontological framework represents a novel methodological crossover.

proposals.title: Establishing Measurement Invariance of the Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI) Across Clinical and Non-Clinical Populations in English-Speaking Countries
proposals.motivation: The Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI) has demonstrated strong psychometric properties in general and student populations in Indonesia and Malaysia. However, its validity for comparing individuals with and without clinical conditions (e.g., eating disorders, severe obesity) remains untested. Establishing measurement invariance is crucial for ensuring that observed group differences in stigma exposure reflect true differences, not measurement bias, which is a prerequisite for meaningful clinical and public health research.
proposals.hypothesis: The WeSEI will demonstrate configural, metric, and scalar measurement invariance across clinical (individuals with diagnosed binge-eating disorder or seeking bariatric surgery) and non-clinical (community sample) groups, indicating that the instrument measures the same construct with the same structure and unit of measurement across these populations.
proposals.method: This cross-sectional study will recruit three groups (N=300 each): a community sample, a clinical sample with binge-eating disorder, and a clinical sample seeking bariatric surgery. Data will be collected via online surveys in English-speaking countries (e.g., UK, US, Australia). Measurement invariance will be tested using multi-group confirmatory factor analysis (MG-CFA) in Mplus or R's lavaan package, following a hierarchical sequence: configural, metric, and scalar invariance. The DIFFTEST procedure will be used for nested model comparisons of the WLSMV estimator, appropriate for ordinal data. Partial invariance will be explored if full invariance is not achieved.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first evidence for the validity of using the WeSEI to compare weight stigma exposure between clinical and non-clinical groups. It will enable researchers to confidently investigate the role of stigma exposure in the etiology and maintenance of clinical conditions. The findings will also inform potential refinements to the WeSEI for specialized use in healthcare settings.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has validated the WeSEI across gender and culture, this is the first proposal to rigorously test its invariance across clinical status, a critical step for its application in health psychology and psychiatry. It extends the methodological focus on 'across' and 'among' groups into the crucial domain of clinical utility.

proposals.title: Psychometric Comparison of Five-Domain and Expanded-Domain Measures of Internalized Weight Stigma: A Multi-Group Study Across Weight Status and Gender
proposals.motivation: Internalized weight stigma (IWS) is a critical psychological construct linked to adverse health outcomes. Pre-2026 research has developed two primary measurement approaches: the five-domain model (e.g., Weight Bias Internalization Scale, WBIS) and expanded-domain instruments that incorporate additional facets like self-perception and social identity. The comparative validity and clinical utility of these competing models remain unclear, creating ambiguity for researchers and clinicians.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an expanded-domain measure of internalized weight stigma will demonstrate superior construct validity (e.g., stronger convergent validity with depression, anxiety, and body image measures) and incremental predictive utility for psychological distress compared to the established five-domain measure, particularly among individuals with higher body mass index.
proposals.method: This cross-sectional study will recruit a large, diverse sample (N > 800) stratified by weight status and gender. Participants will complete the five-domain WBIS, an expanded-domain IWS measure (e.g., incorporating items from the Weight Self-Stigma Questionnaire), and validated measures of psychological distress, self-esteem, and mattering. We will employ confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) to test model fit for each instrument and multi-group CFA to assess measurement invariance across weight status and gender. Hierarchical regression analyses will compare the instruments' incremental predictive validity for key outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first direct, empirical comparison of the two dominant IWS measurement models, offering clear guidance for instrument selection in future research. It will also advance understanding of whether expanded conceptualizations of IWS capture unique, clinically relevant variance, potentially informing more targeted intervention strategies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Griffiths and others has validated specific IWS scales (like the WeSEI for exposure), no pre-2026 study has directly compared the core structural models of IWS measurement using rigorous psychometric methods across diverse subgroups to determine which framework offers greater scientific and clinical value.

proposals.title: Cognitive Diagnostic Modeling of Intrinsic Capacity: Profiling Domain Mastery and Its Association with Weight Stigma Exposure in Older Adults
proposals.motivation: Intrinsic capacity (IC), a core concept of healthy aging, is a multidimensional construct encompassing cognitive, sensory, locomotor, vitality, and psychological domains. Traditional psychometric approaches (e.g., factor analysis, sum scores) treat IC as a continuous latent trait, potentially obscuring distinct, clinically meaningful profiles of domain-specific strengths and deficits. Concurrently, weight stigma is a significant psychosocial stressor known to impact mental and physical health, but its relationship with specific IC profiles in older adults is poorly understood.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying cognitive diagnostic modeling (CDM) to IC assessment data will reveal distinct, interpretable mastery profiles (e.g., 'cognitively resilient, physically vulnerable'), and these profiles will be differentially associated with exposure to weight stigma, independent of overall capacity level.
proposals.method: We will apply established CDM frameworks (e.g., the DINA or GDINA models) to item-level data from a validated IC assessment battery. This involves defining a Q-matrix specifying which IC items (e.g., memory recall, gait speed tests) tap into which underlying domains (attributes). The model will probabilistically classify each older adult participant into a profile of mastery (1) or non-mastery (0) for each IC domain. We will then use latent class or regression analysis to test associations between these CDM-derived profiles and scores on the Weight Stigma Exposure Inventory (WeSEI), controlling for covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, person-centered taxonomy of IC based on domain mastery, moving beyond a single composite score. It will establish a methodological bridge between educational psychometrics and geriatric assessment. Furthermore, it will generate the first empirical evidence linking specific patterns of IC vulnerability to the psychosocial burden of weight stigma, informing targeted interventions.
proposals.novelty: While CDM is well-established in education, its application to profile multidimensional health constructs like intrinsic capacity is novel. Integrating this advanced psychometric method with the study of weight stigma exposure creates a unique intersection of methodological innovation and substantive health psychology research.

ideas.title: Culturally Responsive Validation of the ICOPE Framework: Testing Measurement Invariance and Sensitivity to Intervention Across Diverse Populations
ideas.description: This study integrates cultural validation with intervention responsiveness testing to rigorously evaluate the WHO ICOPE framework. It will employ longitudinal, multi-site designs in culturally diverse non-Western contexts to test for measurement invariance of ICOPE-TW scores before and after evidence-based geriatric interventions. This approach determines if the construct's structure remains equivalent across groups post-intervention, and if score changes reflect true intervention effects rather than cultural measurement bias. The outcome is a validated, cross-culturally fair tool capable of reliably tracking the efficacy of aging care programs worldwide.

ideas.title: Validate and Compare the ICOPES-TW Factor Structure with Multi-Group CFA and Alternative Model Testing
ideas.description: This study rigorously validates the ICOPES-TW's purported two-factor structure by first conducting a multi-group confirmatory factor analysis to establish configural, metric, and scalar measurement invariance across key demographic and clinical subgroups. It then employs a competitive model testing approach, statistically comparing the fit of the hypothesized two-factor model against a set of plausible alternative models, including a unidimensional model and a bifactor model. This design not only confirms model fit in a new sample but provides strong, comparative evidence for the superiority and stability of the intended factor structure. The result is a more robust validation that directly addresses issues of generalizability and model specificity.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Psychometric Validation of the I-PACE Model via Iterative ICOPES-TW Refinement
ideas.description: This study integrates a longitudinal test of the full I-PACE model with a process of iterative psychometric refinement of the ICOPES-TW instrument. Data collected at multiple time points will be used to analyze the model's causal pathways while also assessing and improving the internal consistency and factorial validity of the ICOPES-TW scales. This allows for the revision or addition of items based on empirical longitudinal data, ensuring the measure becomes more sensitive to change and predictive of outcomes over time. Consequently, the evolving instrument enhances the precision of the longitudinal model test, creating a synergistic feedback loop between theory validation and measurement optimization.

ideas.title: Culturally Grounded Evaluation and Augmentation of the ICOPE Framework
ideas.description: This research employs a community-based participatory design to first inductively map local, culturally specific models of healthy aging and capacity in diverse non-Western settings. These emic models are then systematically compared with the etic constructs of the WHO ICOPE framework to identify gaps, mismatches, and points of convergence. The study finally develops and quantitatively validates a culturally augmented version of ICOPE that integrates essential local domains, testing whether this hybrid model demonstrates superior predictive validity for key health and well-being outcomes compared to the standard framework alone.

ideas.title: Applying a Pre-Post Ecological Monitoring Framework to Measure ICOPES-TW Sensitivity to Geriatric Interventions
ideas.description: This research seed is expanded via analogy to ecological monitoring. In environmental science, multi-metric indices are used to detect system-wide changes in ecosystems following restoration interventions. Adapting this solution, the research will treat the older adult's holistic health as a complex system and the ICOPES-TW as its multi-domain integrity index. The study will involve establishing pre-intervention ICOPES-TW baselines for participants, administering a standardized geriatric intervention or care program, and then collecting repeated post-intervention scores. The core analytical approach will be adapted from ecological monitoring to rigorously test whether the magnitude and pattern of score changes are detectably responsive to the intervention, thereby validating ICOPES-TW as a sensitive tool for measuring outcomes in geriatric care and rehabilitation.

ideas.title: Validate ICOPES-TW structure using multi-group confirmatory factor analysis adapted from structural engineering validation
ideas.description: This expanded idea proposes to validate the two-factor structure of the ICOPES-TW instrument using a solution adapted from structural engineering. In that field, theoretical models for composite materials are validated across multiple production batches to ensure robustness. Adapted to this context, the research would employ Multi-Group Confirmatory Factor Analysis (MGCFA) on data collected from two or three distinct sub-samples (e.g., from different hospital systems or regional care networks). This approach goes beyond simple CFA on a single new sample by rigorously testing for measurement invariance across groups. It would confirm whether the two-factor model is consistently applicable and not an artifact of a specific sample, thereby providing stronger evidence for the instrument's structural validity and generalizability in diverse practical settings.

ideas.title: Intensive Longitudinal Case Studies of Intervention Responsiveness in High-Risk Geriatric Patients
ideas.description: This research conducts repeated, intensive mixed-methods case studies on a small number of high-risk older adults (with high WRSS) as they undergo a standardized geriatric intervention or care program. It employs the ICOPES-TW as a frequent, sensitive outcome measure to create a fine-grained temporal map of responsiveness, while deeply contextualizing score fluctuations with concurrent qualitative data (e.g., interviews, observations, clinical notes). This design reveals the idiosyncratic pathways—including personal, social, and clinical mediators—that link intervention components to moments of improvement, stagnation, or decline, thereby generating mechanistic, person-centered hypotheses about intervention effectiveness.

ideas.title: Applying Psychometric Factor Analysis to Deconstruct Intrinsic Capacity
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that intrinsic capacity is a simple two-dimensional construct by drawing an analogy from psychometrics. In intelligence research, the simplistic two-factor model was overturned using factor analysis on diverse test data, revealing a more nuanced hierarchical structure of multiple correlated abilities. Adapting this solution, the research would apply exploratory and confirmatory factor analysis to a broad battery of measures covering all proposed domains of intrinsic capacity. The goal is to statistically determine the optimal latent structure—whether it is unidimensional, bidimensional, or multidimensional—thereby providing an empirical, data-driven foundation for the construct's architecture, moving beyond a priori theoretical assumptions.

ideas.title: Bidirectional Pathways of Wearable-Related Stress and Distress in Clinical and Diverse Populations
ideas.description: This study investigates the bidirectional causal relationships between Wearable-Related Self-Surveillance (WRSS)/problematic use and psychological distress within clinical weight management groups and diverse cultural/age cohorts. By employing longitudinal designs and cross-lagged panel analyses across these specific populations, it tests whether the dominant causal direction (distress-to-use or use-to-distress) differs based on clinical status or sociocultural context. This approach reveals not just if the pathway is bidirectional, but for whom and under what conditions these reinforcing cycles are most pronounced, informing tailored clinical guidelines and intervention points.

ideas.title: Diagnostic Construct Validation and Model-Appropriate Revision of the ICOPES-TW
ideas.description: This study first diagnoses the cause of low internal consistency in the ICOPES-TW by testing its theoretical measurement model (reflective vs. formative/multidimensional) using structural equation modeling and expert qualitative analysis. If the construct is validly multidimensional, the system develops a robust composite index or profile scoring system, rather than forcing high alpha. If items are flawed, it employs a targeted, theory-driven revision process. The final output is a psychometrically justified scale—either a refined reflective scale, a formative index, or a profile tool—validated against key external outcomes to ensure it retains real-world clinical relevance.

ideas.title: Developing a Bidirectional, Psychometrically Robust ICOPES-TW to Test Reciprocal Distress-Use Pathways
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the need for psychometric improvement of the ICOPES-TW with the theoretical challenge to its assumed unidirectional distress-to-use model. The core fused idea is to revise and expand the ICOPES-TW items not just for higher internal consistency, but to explicitly capture the hypothesized bidirectional pathways—creating distinct, reliable subscales that measure distress-as-antecedent and distress-as-consequence of problematic social media use. This psychometrically robust, bidirectional instrument would then be deployed in longitudinal panel studies or ecological momentary assessment (EMA) designs to empirically test for reciprocal, reinforcing cycles between psychological distress and problematic use, moving beyond simple correlation to model dynamic temporal relationships.

ideas.title: Explainable, Causal ML for Geriatric Risk Stratification with ICOPES-TW
ideas.description: This system employs inherently interpretable machine learning models to combine ICOPES-TW data with other clinical and social determinants. It is designed to output not just a risk score, but a clear attribution of which factors (e.g., specific ICOPES domains, lab values, medication counts) drove the prediction and their proposed causal relationship to adverse outcomes. The model's reasoning is validated against a structured knowledge base of geriatric medicine to ensure clinical plausibility. This approach aims to produce actionable, trustworthy predictions that clinicians can use to prioritize and tailor interventions for individual patients.

ideas.title: Digital Phenotyping of Intrinsic Capacity: Uncovering Multidimensional Behavioral Signatures
ideas.description: This research program fuses the conceptual critique of oversimplified intrinsic capacity models with the objective measurement power of digital phenotyping. It proposes using passive smartphone sensing data (e.g., app usage patterns, mobility, communication rhythms, screen time sequences) as a continuous, fine-grained behavioral assay of an individual's functional capacities in their natural environment. By applying advanced dimensionality reduction and latent variable modeling to this rich behavioral dataset, the research aims to empirically identify and validate the number and nature of latent dimensions that constitute intrinsic capacity, such as cognitive resilience, socio-emotional regulation, or motivational drive, which may be obscured in traditional two-factor models. This approach transforms smartphone data from a simple metric of 'problematic use' into a foundational tool for computational gerontology and psychology, enabling a dynamic, multidimensional, and person-specific mapping of capacity.

ideas.title: Reconceptualizing ICOPES-TW as a Formative Index: A Psychometric Redesign to Improve Coherence and Validity
ideas.description: This research synthesizes two approaches by first arguing that the ICOPES-TW should be conceptualized and validated as a formative or index model, where items are causes of the composite construct, not interchangeable reflections of it. Guided by this reconceptualization, the study then systematically revises and adds items to ensure the index comprehensively covers all distinct, necessary facets of the target construct. The synergy produces a psychometrically defensible instrument where traditional low Cronbach's alpha is not a flaw but an expected characteristic of a well-specified formative measure, and improvements focus on enhancing content validity and predictive utility.

ideas.title: Normative Mapping of Digital Resilience: Establishing Population Benchmarks for Positive Body Image Outcomes in Social Media Contexts
ideas.description: This research synthesizes normative psychometric development with the study of positive digital experiences to create a novel assessment framework. It aims to establish population norms and clinically meaningful cut-off scores not for pathology, but for positive body image outcomes (e.g., body appreciation, low stigma) specifically linked to constructive social media use and low distress. This will produce a dual-axis metric that identifies individuals at risk for body image concerns while also benchmarking and defining optimal states of 'digital resilience' across different age groups. The resulting norms will enable public health and clinical initiatives to move beyond harm reduction to actively promote and measure population-level well-being in digital environments.

ideas.title: Disentangling Intervention Effects from Natural Fluctuation: A Stepped-Wedge Trial with Intensive Longitudinal Assessment of ICOPES-TW Responsiveness
ideas.description: This study uses a stepped-wedge cluster randomized design to rigorously test if changes in ICOPES-TW scores are attributable to a geriatric care program. Clusters (e.g., clinical units or communities) are randomly assigned to transition from control to intervention phases at different time points, creating embedded control conditions. Intensive longitudinal sampling of ICOPES-TW scores before, during, and after the transition in each cluster allows for modeling of the change trajectory and comparison to the pre-transition baseline trend. This design controls for secular trends and natural variation, providing robust causal inference about the instrument's responsiveness to the specific intervention.

ideas.title: Contingent Administration Framework: Dynamically Assigning Format Based on Respondent Capability and Context
ideas.description: This research proposes a contingency framework to determine the optimal data collection format, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all claim. It begins with a validated screening protocol that assesses factors like literacy, cognitive capacity, technological access, and clinical status. The system then algorithmically assigns the respondent to interviewer-administered, digitally-assisted, or independent self-report formats based on pre-established rules linking these moderators to format validity. This ensures measurement robustness across the full population spectrum by matching format to capability, directly addressing the exclusion and bias inherent in universal digital or self-report approaches.

ideas.title: Dynamic Psychometric Calibration via Continuous Behavioral Monitoring
ideas.description: This research fuses the concept of continuous monitoring with the goal of improving psychometric scale consistency. It proposes using data streams from wearable sensors and digital phenotyping (e.g., sleep patterns, activity, social interaction) as a continuous, objective 'screening' layer to identify moments of high ecological relevance for administering the ICOPES-TW or similar scales. The temporal correlation and divergence between the continuous behavioral data and discrete scale responses are then analyzed to identify poorly performing or context-dependent items. This enables a data-driven revision process where items are added, removed, or rephrased based on their consistency with real-world behavioral constructs over time, fundamentally improving the scale's internal consistency and ecological validity.

ideas.title: Dynamic Norms and Intervention Thresholds: Using Algorithmic Media Exposure to Calibrate and Validate ICOPES-TW Cut-offs
ideas.description: This research fuses the development of population norms for the ICOPES-TW with an experimental test of non-uniform media exposure. The core innovation is to use controlled exposure to algorithm-curated, positive weight-related content as a 'stress test' or 'buffer test' for the proposed clinical cut-off scores. By measuring how individuals at different percentile ranks (e.g., sub-clinical, near-threshold, clinical) respond to this media intervention, we can determine if the static norms hold under protective conditions or if they need adjustment for an individual's media ecology. This establishes ecologically validated, intervention-informed thresholds that indicate not just current symptom severity, but also likely responsiveness to a specific, scalable digital intervention.

ideas.title: Participatory Co-Validation in Clinical and Community Contexts
ideas.description: This research employs a participatory action research framework to validate the model. For each target population—such as a clinical weight management group or a specific cultural community—a co-design team of end-users and local experts is formed to adapt the model's interface, metrics, and feedback mechanisms. The study then tests these co-adapted variants using a mixed-methods approach, prioritizing qualitative insights and community-defined outcomes alongside standard efficacy measures. This ensures the validation process itself generates actionable, culturally grounded knowledge about the model's real-world utility and limitations.

ideas.title: Adapting Survival Analysis from Engineering to Predict Clinical Trajectories
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from reliability engineering, where survival analysis predicts system failure from initial performance metrics, to the clinical research context. The analogous problem of predicting time-to-failure from stress test scores is solved using statistical survival models that handle censored data. The adapted research idea is to use Kaplan-Meier analysis and Cox proportional hazards regression to assess if baseline ICOPES-TW scores predict the time to future functional decline, hospitalization, or mortality in a longitudinal cohort study, providing a robust, time-to-event framework for predictive validity.

ideas.title: Improve ICOPES-TW Internal Consistency via Psychometric Refinement Inspired by Composite Material Testing
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from materials science, where inconsistent test results for composites are addressed by analyzing the material's microstructure to find and fix fundamental flaws. The analogous problem in psychometrics is the ICOPES-TW's low Cronbach's alpha, indicating poor internal consistency. The adapted research idea is to first conduct a detailed psychometric 'microstructure' analysis (e.g., factor analysis, IRT, cognitive interviews) to diagnose the specific causes—such as item misalignment, ambiguity, or subscale multidimensionality. The solution is then to strategically refine or add items that specifically target these diagnosed weaknesses, thereby strengthening the 'cohesive structure' of the total scale and its subscales, rather than making untargeted revisions.

ideas.title: Establishing ICOPES-TW Norms via Ecological Benchmarking and Stratified Sampling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the method of establishing ecological baselines to the development of normative data for the ICOPES-TW instrument. In environmental science, norms for a healthy ecosystem are established through stratified sampling of representative sites to create reference distributions for key indicators. Analogously, for the ICOPES-TW, we propose a large-scale normative study using stratified random sampling across key demographic groups (age, gender, etc.) to build robust reference score distributions for each sub-population. The solution for defining cut-offs is similarly adapted: just as ecologists compare disturbed sites to reference conditions to set degradation thresholds, we will compare scores from clinically diagnosed populations to the established normative distributions. Using ROC analysis and other statistical techniques, we can then determine optimal cut-off scores that best differentiate typical from clinically significant levels of psychological distress or impairment for each age group, thereby creating a validated benchmarking system for the tool.

ideas.title: Causal Inference and Competing Risk Analysis of ICOPES-TW Thresholds on Clinical Trajectories
ideas.description: This study uses a target trial emulation framework to estimate the causal effect of crossing a high-risk ICOPES-TW threshold on time-to-functional decline, hospitalization, and mortality. It leverages detailed longitudinal electronic health record and claims data to meticulously match individuals who cross the threshold with comparable controls who do not, based on a rich set of time-varying confounders including comorbidities, prior healthcare use, and medication profiles. Survival analysis with competing risks (Fine-Gray models) is employed to estimate cause-specific hazards and cumulative incidence functions, isolating the independent predictive contribution of the score. The final output is an estimate of the controlled direct effect of the ICOPES-TW risk state, providing robust evidence for its clinical utility in risk stratification.

ideas.title: A Network-Validated Factor Structure for I-PACE: Testing a Dynamic Confirmatory Model
ideas.description: This study proposes a novel methodological fusion that integrates confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) with psychometric network analysis to validate and refine theoretical models like ICOPES-TW and I-PACE. Instead of testing a traditional, independent-factor CFA model, we will specify and test a 'network-confirmatory' model where the latent factors are defined by CFA, but the expected dynamic relationships and feedback loops between these factors, as suggested by the I-PACE framework, are explicitly modeled as a within-construct network. This approach simultaneously confirms the proposed factor structure while testing the hypothesized systemic interactions between constructs, offering a more ecologically valid and theoretically rigorous test of the model's architecture as a dynamic system.

ideas.title: Fairness-Aware Computerized Adaptive Testing for ICOPES-TW
ideas.description: This research develops a tailored, adaptive version of the ICOPES-TW that directly addresses risks of algorithmic bias. The system pre-processes the item bank using DIF analysis to identify and mitigate culturally or demographically biased items. During administration, the adaptive algorithm selects items based on both measurement precision and fairness constraints, ensuring equitable assessment across subgroups. The final shortened instrument is then rigorously validated for measurement invariance, ensuring it provides a valid, reliable, and fair estimate of symptom severity for all individuals.

ideas.title: Assessing Positive Body Image as a Protective Factor Against Functional Decline and Adverse Health Outcomes
ideas.description: This study investigates the predictive validity of positive body image, specifically body appreciation and low internalized stigma, for future clinical outcomes. It examines whether individuals who report positive social media engagement and low body-related distress, as potential drivers of positive body image, demonstrate a slower trajectory of functional decline and lower rates of hospitalization and mortality over a longitudinal follow-up period. The research moves beyond assessing risk to test if fostering positive body image could be a viable intervention target for improving long-term health resilience. This directly tests the hypothesis that positive psychological resources in the body domain confer tangible, measurable protection against adverse health events.

ideas.title: Dynamic Intervention Testing: Targeting Protective Factors to Disrupt Bidirectional WRSS-Distress-Use Cycles
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the causal testing of Idea B with the intervention focus of Idea A to investigate whether strengthening specific protective factors (e.g., self-compassion, body functionality appreciation) can disrupt the hypothesized bidirectional cycle where weight-related self-stigma (WRSS) and problematic use (e.g., of food, social media) both contribute to and are exacerbated by psychological distress. The core innovation is using longitudinal, intensive longitudinal (e.g., EMA), or experimental-causal-chain designs to test if an intervention on a protective factor causally weakens the 'distress-to-use' pathway, the 'use-to-distress' pathway, or both. The goal is to move beyond static risk reduction to develop dynamic interventions that target the engine of the cyclical relationship itself.

ideas.title: Adaptive Media Exposure Assessment and Intervention (AMEAI) for Body Image
ideas.description: This research synthesizes Computer Adaptive Testing (CAT) and algorithm-curated positive media exposure into a unified, interactive platform. The system would first use a CAT version of the ICOPES-TW to rapidly and precisely assess an individual's specific body image concerns and vulnerabilities. Based on this real-time diagnostic profile, the platform then immediately delivers a tailored stream of positive, weight-related media content (e.g., body-positive imagery, narratives) selected by a curation algorithm. Crucially, the system can re-administer adaptive test items interspersed with or following the exposure, allowing for direct, within-session measurement of whether the curated content buffers or shifts the initially assessed cognitive-emotional state, creating a closed-loop assessment-intervention tool.

ideas.title: Dynamic EMA Trajectories as Predictors of Clinical Trajectories
ideas.description: This study fuses Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) with longitudinal outcome tracking to test whether the real-time dynamics of distress, stigma, and compensatory social media use predict future functional decline, hospitalization, or mortality more accurately than a single ICOPES-TW baseline score. By capturing micro-fluctuations and stressor reactivity in daily life, the research can identify specific high-risk patterns (e.g., sustained high volatility, failure to recover from distress spikes) that precede macroscopic clinical events. This enables a shift from predicting *who* is at risk to forecasting *when* an individual is entering a high-risk period, based on their evolving digital phenotyping.

ideas.title: Apply psychometric IRT for adaptive psychological assessment refinement
ideas.description: This idea applies the analogous solution from clinical psychology and psychometrics, where Item Response Theory (IRT) powers Computerized Adaptive Testing (CAT) for efficient psychological assessment. The analogous solution uses IRT to model item parameters and a respondent's latent trait, then algorithmically selects the most informative next item. Adapted to a broader research context, this approach would involve using IRT to model items for latent constructs like organizational attitudes or consumer preferences, then implementing an adaptive algorithm for survey administration. This adaptation aims to significantly improve item selection efficiency and scaling precision while minimizing the number of items each respondent must answer, transferring a mature methodological solution from one measurement field to another.

ideas.title: A Causal-Interventionist Longitudinal Test of the I-PACE Model Pathways
ideas.description: This study employs a hybrid longitudinal design that combines observational tracking with embedded, staggered micro-interventions targeting specific components of the I-PACE model (e.g., affective regulation, cognitive biases). By introducing controlled, ethical perturbations at different time points for random subsets of the cohort, the design creates natural experiments within the longitudinal flow. This allows for testing not just temporal precedence but also manipulation-based causality of the pathways linking predisposing factors, affective/cognitive responses, and compulsive internet-use outcomes. The multi-wave data will be analyzed using advanced causal inference methods to disentangle the strength and direction of each proposed mechanistic link.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling for Clinical and Demographic Validation
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from Ecology, this idea adapts the solution of testing ecological niche models across distinct biogeographic realms to improve transferability. The analogous solution involves validating models in deliberately different, non-analog environments to identify failure modes. Adapted to the clinical research context, the idea is to test the weight management model in carefully selected, highly distinct clinical and demographic groups (e.g., clinical weight management cohorts, diverse cultural or age groups) treated as separate 'niches.' The research would formally assess performance, pinpoint the contextual factors causing prediction drift, and use these insights to refine the model for robust generalizability across diverse human populations, mirroring how ecologists improve species distribution models for global applicability.

ideas.title: Network Intervention Mapping for Weight-Related Self-Stigma: Targeting Protective Factors in the I-PACE System
ideas.description: This research applies psychometric network analysis to model the I-PACE components (e.g., predisposing variables, affective/cognitive responses, executive functions) as an interconnected system that maintains weight-related self-stigma. It then integrates this network model with the search for protective factors, not just as isolated variables, but as potential 'shock absorbers' or 'circuit breakers' within the network. The synergy allows for the computational testing of intervention hypotheses—simulating how strengthening specific protective nodes (or edges) could maximally reduce the connectivity and activation of core stigma-related constructs, thereby designing theoretically precise, system-aware interventions to reduce problematic use and its associated stigma.

ideas.title: Dynamic, Personalized Risk Stratification Using ML-Refined Normative Data for ICOPES-TW
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the development of population norms for ICOPES-TW with machine learning-based risk stratification. Instead of applying ML to raw scores with fixed cut-offs, the models will be trained to interpret ICOPES-TW scores relative to continuously updated, multi-dimensional normative benchmarks (e.g., by age, gender, comorbidities). This allows the algorithms to identify clinically significant deviations specific to an individual's reference group. The resulting system provides dynamic, personalized risk predictions that are more interpretable and clinically actionable, as risk thresholds are context-aware and evolve with new normative data.
